A key finding in previous studies in discourse analysis is that most sentences have an independent well-formed sub-tree in the full document-level discourse tree (Joty et al., 2012). Researchers have taken advantage of this finding to build parsers that utilize different granularity levels of the document to achieve the state-of-the-art results (Kobayashi et al., 2020). This design has been empirically verified to be a generally advantageous framework, improving not only works using traditional feature engineering (Joty et al., 2013;Wang et al., 2017), but also deep learning models (Jia et al., 2018b;Kobayashi et al., 2020). We, therefore, introduce this design to our dependency parsing framework. Specifically, sub-trees for each sentence in a discourse are first built separately, then assembled to form a complete discourse tree. However, our model differs from prior works in that we make a clear distinction to derive better contextualized representations of EDUs from fine-tuning BERT separately for intra-and intersentential levels to dynamically capture different information needed for discourse analysis at different levels. We are also the first to design stacked sequence labeling models for discourse relation identification so that its hierarchical structure can explicitly capture both intra-sentential and intersentential writing patterns.

In the case of implicit relations between EDUs without clear connectives, it is crucial to introduce sequential information from the context to resolve ambiguity. Feng and Hirst (2014) rely on linearchain CRF with traditional feature engineering to make use of the sequential characteristics of the context for discourse constituent parsing. However, they greedily build up the discourse structure and relations from bottom up. At each timestep, they apply the CRF to obtain the locally optimized structure and relation. In this way, the model assigns relation gradually along with the construction of the parsing tree from bottom up, but only limited contextual information from the top level of the partially constructed tree can be used to predict relations. Besides, at each time-step, they sequentially assign relations to top nodes of the partial tree, without being aware that those nodes might represent different levels of discourse units (e.g. EDUs, sentences, or even paragraphs). In contrast, we explicitly train our sequence labeling models on both intra-and inter-sentential levels after a complete discourse tree is constructed so that we can infer from the whole context with a clear intention of capturing different writing patterns occurring at intra-and inter-sentential levels. Alongside this line of work, recent neural discourse parsers have explored multi-task setups with rhetorical structure prediction, sentiment cues, and topic segmentation, and multiple studies have cataloged genre-specific signals in newswire, forums, and scientific writing without harmonizing these signals into a unified modeling hypothesis (Li et al., 2016; Braud et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2018; Peters et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). These efforts span diverse supervision regimes and annotation guidelines, and they include investigations of cross-lingual transfer, domain adaptation, and representation sharing across layers of a transformer encoder; at the same time, related work on hierarchical attention for long documents examines scaling issues and positional encodings (Guzmán et al., 2019; Koto et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). A survey of discourse signals in dialogue and multi-party conversation emphasizes turn-taking features and pragmatic markers in task-oriented interactions, while studies on summarization leverage nucleus-satellite cues to improve content selection (Asher et al., 2016; Li et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). Prior work also evaluates cue scarcity in low-resource genres and investigates curriculum learning for relation labeling, and several datasets revise relation taxonomies while expanding span boundary conventions in multilingual corpora (Biran and McKeown, 2015; Morey et al., 2018; Kobayashi et al., 2019).

These efforts span diverse supervision regimes and annotation guidelines, and they include investigations of cross-lingual transfer, domain adaptation, and representation sharing across layers of a transformer encoder; at the same time, related work on hierarchical attention for long documents examines scaling issues and positional encodings (Guzmán et al., 2019; Koto et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). A survey of discourse signals in dialogue and multi-party conversation emphasizes turn-taking features and pragmatic markers in task-oriented interactions, while studies on summarization leverage nucleus-satellite cues to improve content selection (Asher et al., 2016; Li et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020).

Prior work also evaluates cue scarcity in low-resource genres and investigates curriculum learning for relation labeling, and several datasets revise relation taxonomies while expanding span boundary conventions in multilingual corpora (Biran and McKeown, 2015; Morey et al., 2018; Kobayashi et al., 2019).

document: Text Knowledge enhanced methods. Recently, huge efforts on integrating knowledge into PTLMs have been made. One typical form of knowledge is a knowledge graph. There have been efforts of using knowledge graph to inject entity and relation representations, which are pre-computed from external source, into PTLMs (Zhang et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019;He et al., 2020). Some other works try to retrieve or generate the sub-graph from the graph to solve the problem (Lin et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020). Another existing form of knowledge is extra large-scale corpus. Works that use such corpus present knowledge-related pre-training objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020) and source of knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). They are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Recent studies on multi-modal transformers investigate cross-attention fusion between textual and visual streams (Chen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022) without clarifying the dependency on prior textual pre-training. To situate our work, we review methods that augment PTLMs with structured and unstructured knowledge. Early approaches relied on fixed knowledge graph embeddings that were concatenated or gated into encoder layers. Subsequent methods developed retrieval components that identify task-relevant subgraphs at inference time. A parallel line of research introduced continual pretraining on domain corpora with entity-aware masking and span objectives. These strategies improved factual recall and entity disambiguation in information extraction and question answering. Nevertheless, they often neglect perceptual or procedural knowledge that is not well captured by text alone. Our study targets this gap by aligning textual representations with signals grounded in images and physical interactions. We consider knowledge graphs that encode affordances and spatial relations derived from annotated visual datasets. We also examine corpora that describe manipulation steps and outcomes in instructional narratives. By combining discrete relational structure with sequence-level descriptions, we aim to enhance reasoning about actions and object states. We prioritize approaches that remain parameter-efficient and compatible with standard PTLM checkpoints. Concretely, we extend a retrieval-augmented encoder with a lightweight adapter for multi-source evidence. The adapter conditions token representations on graph neighborhoods and visual descriptors. We then train with a curriculum that alternates between factual queries and commonsense action queries. This design encourages the model to unify symbolic links with perceptual cues. For evaluation, we adopt tasks that require grounded inference, including procedural QA and visual commonsense QA. We report metrics on answer accuracy, calibration, and retrieval faithfulness. In ablations, we isolate the contribution of graph structure, visual features, and corpus pretraining. Results indicate that joint conditioning yields consistent gains over text-only baselines. Error analysis shows improvements in object state transitions and tool-usage predictions. We also observe better robustness to distractor entities during retrieval. Finally, we discuss implications for scalable knowledge integration without exhaustive end-to-end finetuning. We release code, resources, and templates for reproducible experiments. Our contributions include a unified adapter, a mixed-source curriculum, and a benchmark protocol for grounded knowledge assessment. We anticipate broader impact in applications that require safe and reliable reasoning about the physical world. Future work will explore automatic curation of affordance graphs from weakly labeled images. We also plan to investigate continual updating mechanisms that preserve alignment across modalities. By advancing coherence between structured knowledge and perceptual signals, our approach moves PTLMs toward richer commonsense understanding.
spans: There have been efforts of using knowledge graph to inject entity and relation representations, which are pre-computed from external source, into PTLMs (Zhang et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019;He et al., 2020). Some other works try to retrieve or generate the sub-graph from the graph to solve the problem (Lin et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020). Another existing form of knowledge is extra large-scale corpus.

Works that use such corpus present knowledge-related pre-training objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020) and source of knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). They are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Recent studies on multi-modal transformers investigate cross-attention fusion between textual and visual streams (Chen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022) without clarifying the dependency on prior textual pre-training.

Cross-lingual/Multilingual Machine Reading Comprehension Hsu et al. (2019) investigate cross-lingual transfer capability of multilingual BERT (mBERT) on MRC tasks and find that zeroshot learning based on PLM is feasible, even between distant languages, such as English and Chinese. Various approaches have been proposed on top of multilingual MRC based on PLMs. Cui et al. (2019) propose a method that combines multilingual BERT and back-translation for cross-lingual MRC. In order to effectively leverage translation Different from the above studies, we mainly consider the impact of syntactic divergences between the source and target language in zero-shot crosslingual transfer based on multilingual PLMs, and attempt to disassociate semantics from syntax and only transfer semantic to the target language.

Disentangled Representation Learning Recently, there has been a growing amount of work on learning disentangled latent representations in NLP tasks (Zhang et al., 2019;Hu et al., 2017;Yin et al., 2018). In this aspect, the most related work to our syntax-semantics decoupling method is the vMF-Gaussian Variational Autoencoder (VGVAE) model proposed by Chen et al. (2019). It is a generative model using two latent variables to represent semantics and syntax of the sentence, developed for monolingual setting and trained with paraphrases. It uses paraphrase reconstruction loss and a discriminative paraphrase loss to learn semantic representations and word order information for syntactic representations. We adapt this model to multilingual syntax-semantics disentanglement. We use bilingual sentence pairs to train our model with a cross-lingual reconstruction loss and semantic discrimination loss. To better disentangle semantics from complex and diverse syntax in multilingual PLMs, we introduce two additional syntax-related losses for incorporating POS tags and syntax trees. Recent advances in question answering under multilingual settings emphasize that pretrained encoders capture cross-lingual signals that can be surfaced with probing tasks (Artetxe et al., 2020; Pires et al., 2019; Conneau and Lample, 2019). Large-scale parallel corpora have been curated for evaluation in machine reading comprehension, often contrasting extractive and generative answer formats (Lewis et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). Tokenization granularity has also been examined as a factor in transfer efficiency, with subword regularization reported to increase robustness across scripts (Kudo, 2018). We position our work at the intersection of cross-lingual MRC and disentangled representation learning. Our primary goal is to assess whether separating syntactic and semantic factors improves zero-shot transfer on extractive MRC benchmarks. We contribute a training framework that jointly optimizes cross-lingual reconstruction and semantic discrimination while regularizing syntax via POS and dependency cues. We further analyze the role of syntactic distance between language pairs in modulating transfer gains. Our experiments span typologically diverse languages to stress-test the decoupling assumptions. We report detailed ablations over loss components, bilingual data sources, and alignment strategies. The results indicate that explicit syntax-guided disentanglement yields consistent improvements over strong mBERT and XLM-R baselines. We also find that the approach remains robust when parallel data is noisy or partially misaligned. Error analysis suggests that improvements are concentrated on questions involving long-distance dependencies and head-final structures. We release code, pretrained checkpoints, and training scripts to facilitate reproducibility. We additionally provide a multilingual diagnostic set targeting syntactic phenomena relevant to MRC. The diagnostic probes include control for answer length and clause embedding depth. We discuss limitations related to reliance on external taggers and parsers in low-resource settings. Future work may reduce supervision by leveraging unsupervised or weakly supervised syntax induction. This line of research could help unify cross-lingual transfer with structure-aware pretraining. Ultimately, our findings underscore the value of decoupling syntax from semantics for robust multilingual comprehension. By clarifying the contribution of structure, we aim to inform the design of next-generation multilingual PLMs. We conclude with open questions on scaling disentanglement to generative MRC and dialogue-oriented reading tasks.
spans: Recent advances in question answering under multilingual settings emphasize that pretrained encoders capture cross-lingual signals that can be surfaced with probing tasks (Artetxe et al., 2020; Pires et al., 2019; Conneau and Lample, 2019). Large-scale parallel corpora have been curated for evaluation in machine reading comprehension, often contrasting extractive and generative answer formats (Lewis et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). Tokenization granularity has also been examined as a factor in transfer efficiency, with subword regularization reported to increase robustness across scripts (Kudo, 2018).

Multiple efforts have looked into defining, measuring, and mitigating biases in NLP models (Sun et al., 2019;Mehrabi et al., 2019a;Sheng et al., 2021). Dwork et al. (2012) provide foundational definitions of fairness that have informed subsequent metric design in machine learning. Multiple recent works also focus on developing new dataset and associated metrics to capture various types of biases. For example,  and Nangia et al. (2020) propose dataset and metrics to measure social biases and stereotypes in language model generations, Bolukbasi et al. (2016); Caliskan et al. (2017); Manzini et al. (2019) define metrics to access gender and race biases in word vector representations, and  define metrics to quantify and mitigate biases in visual recognition task. Ethayarajh (2020) propose Bernstein bounds to represent uncertainty about the bias. Majority of these bias metrics are automatically computed, for example, using a regard classifier (Sheng et al., 2019), sentiment classifier , toxicity classifier (Dixon et al., 2018) or true positive rate difference between privileged and underprivileged groups (De-Arteaga et al., 2019b). A few works additionally validate the alignment of these automatically computed bias metrics with human understanding of biases by collecting annotations of biases on a subset of test data from crowdworkers (Sheng et al., 2019;. Blodgett et al. (2021Blodgett et al. ( , 2020 discuss the limitations of several of these bias datasets and measurements.

Recent work examines bias in pretrained language models via counterfactual data augmentation (Zmigrod et al., 2019), intrinsic association tests (May et al., 2019), and causal probing frameworks (Vig et al., 2020). Hovy and Spruit (2016) discuss social impacts of NLP technology and dataset documentation practices. Bender and Friedman (2018) introduce a data statement protocol for transparency, and Buolamwini and Gebru (2018) study demographic disparities in face recognition performance.

However, the majority of existing bias metrics are specific to the model type and the application domain used, they may not be tested for correlation with human judgement of biases, and their relationship to existing definitions of fairness has not been explored. Additionally, metrics such as true positive or error difference between groups requires ground truth labels, thereby making their computation in real-time systems difficult. Speicher et al. (2018) have attempted to present unified approach to measuring group and individual fairness via inequality indices, however we note that such metrics are non-trivial to extend to unstructured data such as text. For example, gender information in a text may be subtle (e.g. mention of softball) and it is unclear whether presence of this word should be considered to impact the genderness of the text. Accumulated prediction sensitivity metric, presented in this paper, attempts to address all the above limitations of existing bias metrics. We acknowledge that the proposed metric is yet to be associated with other notions of fairness (e.g. preference based notion of fairness (Zafar et al., 2017)).
spans: Recent work examines bias in pretrained language models via counterfactual data augmentation (Zmigrod et al., 2019), intrinsic association tests (May et al., 2019), and causal probing frameworks (Vig et al., 2020). Hovy and Spruit (2016) discuss social impacts of NLP technology and dataset documentation practices. Bender and Friedman (2018) introduce a data statement protocol for transparency, and Buolamwini and Gebru (2018) study demographic disparities in face recognition performance.

Several approaches to zero-shot cross-lingual transfer exist and can broadly be divided into: a) Databased Transfer, which focuses on training data transformation and b) Model-based Transfer that centres around modifying models' training routine.

Data-based Transfer Translating utterances for the intent classification task is relatively straightforward so previous works focused on projecting and/or aligning the entity labels between translated utterances (Dou and Neubig, 2021). This technique is followed by standard supervised training with those pseudo-labels and is commonly known as a translate-train method. One of the earliest works still being used for this purpose is fastalign (Dyer et al., 2013). It's an unsupervised word aligner trained on a parallel corpus to map each word (thus its entity label) in the source utterance to the word(s) in the target user utterance. Projecting the entity labels can also be done with word-by-word translation and source label copying (Yi and Cheng, 2021). A teacher model then weakly labels the target data, which is used to train the final student model. Sometimes, this type of label projection is complemented with an additional entity alignment step (Li et al., 2021a). Better performance can be achieved by using machine translation with entity matching and distributional statistics (Jain et al., 2019) though this can be a costly process for each language. A category of 'word substitution' methods such as code-switching (Qin et al., 2020;Kuwanto et al., 2021) or dictionary-enhanced pretraining have also been shown to improve cross-lingual transfer.

Model-based Transfer Prior to the adoption of multilingual transformers (Lample and Conneau, 2019), task-oriented XNLU methods employed a BiLSTM encoder combined with different multilingual embeddings (Schuster et al., 2019). Newer approaches usually involve a pretrained XLM and the addition of some new training component with the inference routine remaining mostly unchanged. Xu et al. (2020) learn to jointly align and predict entity labels by fusing the source and target language embeddings with attention and using the resulting cross-lingual representation for entity prediction. Qi and Du (2020) include an adversarial language detector in training whose loss encourages the model to generate language-agnostic sentence representations for improved zero-shot trans-fer. Pan et al. (2020) and Chi et al. (2020) added a contrastive loss to pretraining that treats translated sentences as positive examples and unrelated sentences as negative samples. This training step helps the XLM produce similar embeddings in different languages. However, these methods require large annotated datasets and expensive model pretraining (Chi et al., 2020). Our proposed methods only use the English task data (which is relatively limited) and its translations for each language.

The most related prior works are Arivazhagan et al. (2019) for machine translation and XeroAlign for task-oriented XNLU. Both of these are cross-lingual alignment methods that use translated training data to zero-shot transfer the source language model to the target language. We focus on the latter work, called XeroAlign, which reported the most recent SOTA scores on our evaluation datasets. XeroAlign works by generating a sentence embedding of the user utterance for each language, e.g. English (source) and Thai (target) using the CLS token of the XLM. A Mean Squared Error loss function minimises the difference between the multilingual sentence embeddings and is backpropagated along with the main task loss. XeroAlign aims to bring sentence embeddings in different languages closer together with a bias towards intent classification due to the CLS embedding, which is the standard input to the intent classifier. We reproduce this method for analysis and comparisons but add a small post-processing step that distinctly improves the reported scores.

Recent studies span diverse transfer paradigms in multilingual NLP, including prompt-based adaptation that conditions pretrained decoders on language tags (Winata et al., 2021), curriculum strategies that schedule target-language exposure during fine-tuning (Conneau et al., 2020), and lexicon-grounded objectives that inject bilingual signal through dictionary constraints (Artetxe et al., 2018). Multilingual pretraining scales to hundreds of languages with shared subword vocabularies and masked language modeling, and encoder-decoder models can be distilled into compact student networks for on-device use (Sanh et al., 2019). Signal alignment in speech-to-text pipelines also benefits from shared bottleneck representations between acoustic and textual modalities (Pratap et al., 2021).
spans: Recent studies span diverse transfer paradigms in multilingual NLP, including prompt-based adaptation that conditions pretrained decoders on language tags (Winata et al., 2021), curriculum strategies that schedule target-language exposure during fine-tuning (Conneau et al., 2020), and lexicon-grounded objectives that inject bilingual signal through dictionary constraints (Artetxe et al., 2018). Multilingual pretraining scales to hundreds of languages with shared subword vocabularies and masked language modeling, and encoder-decoder models can be distilled into compact student networks for on-device use (Sanh et al., 2019). Signal alignment in speech-to-text pipelines also benefits from shared bottleneck representations between acoustic and textual modalities (Pratap et al., 2021).

document: This section reports a survey on closely related popular QA datasets that 1) focus on narratives and/or 2) are designed for educational purposes (dataset features in Table ) 1 . We begin by delineating narrative-centric QA from sentence-level reading comprehension, emphasizing discourse structure and event temporality. Narrative datasets often evaluate cross-sentence reasoning, coreference, and character modeling across long contexts. Educational QA corpora, by contrast, foreground curricular alignment, cognitive skill coverage, and item difficulty calibration. Prior work has cataloged these dimensions to clarify where datasets stress comprehension versus retrieval. Prior work on narrative QA is often contrasted with reading comprehension benchmarks that emphasize sentence-level fact retrieval; Hermann et al. (2015) introduced cloze-style queries over news, Rajpurkar et al. (2016) framed extractive QA on Wikipedia, and Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) scaled open-domain answering with heterogeneous sources. Hill et al. (2016) collected the bAbI tasks to test reasoning with synthetic stories. Most narrative QA settings instead require modeling long-range dependencies across events and characters in realistic texts. The BookTest corpus (Bajgar et al., 2016) extended cloze queries to novels. NarrativeQA (Kocisky et al., 2018) paired plot summaries and full books/films with abstractive questions and answers. DREAM (Sun et al., 2019) targeted multi-turn dialogue comprehension in exams. SQuAD-style adversarial sets have also probed robustness. Educational question generation has investigated didactic scaffolding and item difficulty. TextbookQA (Kembhavi et al., 2017) aligned multimodal textbook content with questions. RACE (Lai et al., 2017) aggregated English examination questions from Chinese middle and high schools. DuoRC (Saha et al., 2018) introduced paraphrase-heavy movie synopses requiring inference. To ground the survey, we first group datasets by narrative source: literary fiction, film synopses, dialogues, and news-derived cloze narratives. We then articulate educational orientation along three axes: assessment context, annotation policy, and cognitive taxonomy. For literary fiction, NarrativeQA stands out for its dual setting (summary versus full text) that separates surface recall from deep narrative understanding. For film materials, MovieQA (Tapaswi et al., 2016) integrates plots, subtitles, and videos, enabling multimodal narrative reasoning. DuoRC focuses on paraphrastic divergence between documents and questions, intentionally reducing lexical overlaps. For dialogues, DREAM tests pragmatic inference and speaker attribution in multi-turn contexts. For news-style narratives, CNN/DailyMail variants remain useful for cloze-based sequence modeling. Turning to education-focused corpora, RACE provides multiple-choice items spanning literal, inferential, and summarization skills. TextbookQA layers diagrams and figures with explanatory passages to reflect classroom resources. ARC (Clark et al., 2018) contrasts easy versus challenge science questions to probe knowledge integration. SciQ (Welbl et al., 2017) offers crowdsourced science items with distractors designed to mimic classroom exams. MCTest (Richardson et al., 2013) supplies small, carefully controlled stories for elementary-level comprehension. TOEFL Listening Comprehension sets introduce authentic academic discourse for non-native learners. Several datasets bridge both aims: QuAC (Choi et al., 2018) and CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019) model conversational comprehension while retaining educational relevance. TREC-style QA remains a reference point for taxonomy and evaluation protocol design. MultiRC (Khashabi et al., 2018) emphasizes multi-sentence reasoning with calibrated distractors. As evaluation practices evolve, leaderboard saturation has motivated adversarial splits and length-controlled partitions. We synthesize these developments by highlighting implications for modeling architectures, annotation design, and curricular validity. The survey concludes with recommendations for dataset selection aligned to narrative complexity and instructional goals.

(2015) introduced cloze-style queries over news, Rajpurkar et al. (2016) framed extractive QA on Wikipedia, and Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) scaled open-domain answering with heterogeneous sources. Hill et al. (2016) collected the bAbI tasks to test reasoning with synthetic stories. Most narrative QA settings instead require modeling long-range dependencies across events and characters in realistic texts. The BookTest corpus (Bajgar et al., 2016) extended cloze queries to novels. NarrativeQA (Kocisky et al., 2018) paired plot summaries and full books/films with abstractive questions and answers. 2.1 Pre-trained language model (PLM)

Most modern language models are based on the transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture. The PLMs generally use only the encoder block of the transformer, which consists of two sublayers, a self-attention layer, and a feed-forward layer. BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) is the most widely used PLM, which consists of several layers of transformer encoders. The BERT was pre-trained for 4 days with a large amount of text data, which consists of 3.3 billion words, using masked language modeling and the next sentence prediction task in an unsupervised manner. This pre-trained parameter can be easily used in various downstream tasks by fine-tuning the model using the labeled dataset. Since the success of BERT, a variety of similar PLMs have emerged.

Explainable classification Unlike post-hoc analysis approaches for explainability (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Sundararajan et al., 2017), prototype classification networks (Li et al., 2018;Hase et al., 2019) are white-box models with explainability built-in via case-based reasoning (Kolodner, 1992) rather than extractive rationales (Lei et al., 2016;Bastings et al., 2019;Jain et al., 2020;Glockner et al., 2020). They are the neural variant of prototype classifiers (Bien and Tibshirani, 2011;Kim et al., 2014), predicting based on similar known instances. Contemporary work (Rajagopal et al., 2021) also stressed the importance of "global" explainability through training examples, yet in their approach, the similar training examples are not directly integrated in the decision itself; in contrast, we do so via learned prototypes to provide more transparency.

Our work builds on Li et al. (2018), which we lay out in Section 3.1. Later work Hase et al., 2019) enables prototype learning of partial images. In NLP, Guu et al. (2018) retrieved prototype examples from the training data for edit-based natural langauge generation. Hase and Bansal (2020) used a variant of 's work to examine among other approaches; unlike our work, they used feature activation to obtain explanations similar to post-hoc approaches, and did not handle the absence of relevant content.

Evaluating explainability Explainability is a multi-faceted problem. HCI concerns include: a) For whom are we designing the explanations? b) What goals are they trying to achieve? c) How can we best convey information without imposing excessive cognitive load? and d) Can explainable systems foster more effective human+AI partnerships (Amershi et al., 2019;Wickramasinghe et al., 2020;Liao et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021;Bansal et al., 2021)? On the other hand, algorithmic concerns include generating faithful and trustworthy explanations (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), local vs. global explanations, and post-hoc vs. self-explanations (Danilevsky et al., 2020).

Explainability evaluation methods (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017) include measuring faithfulness (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), enabling model simulatability (Hase et al., 2019), behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020, and evaluating intelligent user interactions (Nguyen et al., 2018). Recent advances in prompt-based tuning highlight the role of instruction-following models for controllable explanations (Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). Large language model alignment techniques prioritize helpfulness and harmlessness, often measured with preference data (Bai et al., 2022). Self-consistency decoding has been used to improve reasoning reliability on chain-of-thought benchmarks (Wang et al., 2023). Benchmark suites for interpretability stress-test saliency robustness and sparsity. Practical deployments report mixed evidence about whether explanations improve decision quality in high-stakes domains. Some studies frame explanation as a communication problem rather than a transparency problem. There is also growing interest in hybrid symbolic-neural rationalizers that combine program sketches with natural language. Foundational work on causal abstraction formalizes relationships between mechanistic and functional models (Beckers and Halpern, 2019). Crowdsourcing protocols calibrate user trust through contrastive and counterfactual exemplars. Study designs vary in task complexity, time pressure, and prior knowledge. Quality metrics often combine subjective ratings with objective task performance and error detection. Several datasets include rationale annotations that may bias models toward extractive patterns. User mental models evolve over repeated interactions, complicating between-subject comparisons.

Human+AI fake news detection While explainable fact-checking (Kotonya and Toni, 2020b) could better support human-in-the-loop factchecking (Nakov et al., 2021;Demartini et al., 2020), studies rarely assess a human+AI team in combination (Nguyen et al., 2018). In fact, hu-man+AI teams often under-perform the human or AI working alone (Bansal et al., 2021), emphasizing the need to carefully baseline performance.

Propaganda detection (Da San Martino et al., 2019) occupies the broader space of detecting disinformation. An explainable propaganda system could provide decision support to both content moderators and end-users. Because propaganda detection is hard task for non-expert users and the stateof-the-art models are not accurate enough for practical use, explainability may help in larger adoption of computational propaganda detection systems (Martino et al., 2020). Our approach of building a white box model with human evaluation -rooted in an human+AI partnership -aims to bring automated models closer to practical adoption.

There has been the argument revolving deep learning models that they are black-box and hard to offer explainable and interpretable predictions, which has led to a considerable amount of research work regarding their interpretability (Linardatos et al., 2021). The pre-trained BERT language model has been extensively analyzed since its launch, in particular, feature-based probes have been proposed to show how a particular layer, head, or neuron of BERT works on a downstream NLP task. Usually with a small set of additional parameters, a probe is trained in a supervised manner using feature representations from the pre-trained BERT, e.g. contextualized embeddings, to solve a particular task (Wu et al., 2020). Attention and structural probes have been invented to investigate different aspects of BERT and linguistics (Lin et al., 2019;Jawahar et al., 2019;Clark et al., 2019;Hewitt and Manning, 2019;Manning et al., 2020;Tenney et al., 2019a,b;Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). Latent ontology of contextual embeddings has also been investigated via cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020). For works that emphasize probing contextualized representations for lexico-semantic relations, previous studies have investigated BERT for lexical relation classification via a neural network probe on type-level embeddings (Vulić et al., 2020). Researchers have also examined multilingual adaptations of BERT, domain-specific pretraining for biomedical and legal text, and prompt-based steering of masked language models to elicit latent relational knowledge (Devlin et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2019; Chalkidis et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019); knowledge graph completion methods compare structured inference against distributional cues, and few-shot prompting can expose parametric facts without fine-tuning (Bosselut et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Brown et al., 2020). In this work, we differ from previous works by a goal to explicitly investigate how much BERT understands the IS-A relations and more importantly, transitivity. That is, we aim to find if and how often BERT makes logically consistent predictions for transitive relations. Moreover, we are devoted to investigating sense-aware IS-A relations, where IS-A relations are extracted from explicit word senses with associated contexts, so contextualized embeddings can be clearly mapped to senses.
spans: Researchers have also examined multilingual adaptations of BERT, domain-specific pretraining for biomedical and legal text, and prompt-based steering of masked language models to elicit latent relational knowledge (Devlin et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2019; Chalkidis et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019); knowledge graph completion methods compare structured inference against distributional cues, and few-shot prompting can expose parametric facts without fine-tuning (Bosselut et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Brown et al., 2020).

Introduction

Pretrained LM in Open-domain dialogue has received sustained attention as models have scaled and datasets diversified for multi-turn interaction research. Many prior works tried to pretrain the models on large-scale social comment chains data like Reddit to model conversational behavior (Zhang et al., 2020; Adiwardana et al., 2020), followed by finetuning on the diverse target dialogue dataset to improve engagingness and humanness. Prior work emphasizes large-scale pretraining on conversational corpora to endow models with discourse competence and pragmatic flexibility (Roller et al., 2021). Smith et al. (2019) introduced a multi-turn response selection benchmark focusing on context coherence and persona consistency. Zhang et al. (2018) proposed PersonalDialog to capture speaker traits and long-range dependencies across sessions. While these efforts improved surface-level fluency, recent studies highlight shortcomings in factual grounding and safety in open-domain settings (Shuster et al., 2021; Dinan et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented dialogue aims to mitigate hallucination by conditioning generation on retrieved passages from web or curated knowledge sources (Lewis et al., 2020; Komeili et al., 2022). Safety-aware data construction further introduces refusal behaviors and sensitive-topic handling to reduce harmful outputs during both pretraining and finetuning (Baheti et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2022). Instruction tuning with preference optimization has emerged to better align chat models with conversational goals while maintaining factuality and politeness (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). To avoid undesired behaviors of the models including toxicity and bias from the human-human conversation, they merely exclude some parts of training data using automatic filtering by predefined criteria. Despite measurable gains, our work argues that filtering alone is insufficient without explicit safety objectives and grounding signals integrated throughout the training pipeline.
spans: Prior work emphasizes large-scale pretraining on conversational corpora to endow models with discourse competence and pragmatic flexibility (Roller et al., 2021). Smith et al. (2019) introduced a multi-turn response selection benchmark focusing on context coherence and persona consistency. Zhang et al. (2018) proposed PersonalDialog to capture speaker traits and long-range dependencies across sessions.

Quality Estimation Current SOTA models in sentence-level QE, which is typically framed as a regression task, mainly use multilingual representations from pre-trained transformers (Devlin et al., 2018), notably XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2019). The input to a sentence-level QE model is a concatenation of the source and translated sentences, separated by the [SEP] token. The sequence is encoded by the pre-trained Transformer model, and the [CLS] token is passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) layer to obtain a sentence-level score. During fine-tuning both the parameters of the pre-trained model and the parameters corresponding to the MLP layer are updated.

Word-level QE is typically addressed as a binary classification task, where the QE model needs to predict a binary label indicating whether a word is correct or wrong for each word in the MT output (Lee, 2020). As illustrated in Figure  (left), some supervised approaches use both sentence-level and word-level objectives in a multi-task setting, which results in superior performance (Kim et al., 2017;Lee, 2020). Methods that do not require word-level training data either need access to the MT model (Rikters and Fishel, 2017;Fomicheva et al., 2020b), or still treat the problem as a supervised task but use synthetically generated data for supervision (Tuan et al., 2021).

Rationale Extraction for NLP SOTA NLP models based on deep neural networks achieve high performance in a variety of tasks, often at the cost of interpretability (Lipton, 2016). Recent work aims to address this issue by focusing on two different goals. On the one hand, the aim is to produce justifications for model predictions that are plausible to the users, in order to increase users' trust (Ribeiro et al., 2016). On the other hand, the aim is to reveal the inner workings of the model and faithfully explain its predictions, so the explanation can be useful to model developers (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020).

Typically, explainability methods operate by selecting a portion of the input that justifies model prediction for a single data point. This can be done either by modifying the model architecture, or by trying to explain the predictions of a given model. The first type of approaches (a.k.a. rationalization by construction) involves imposing restrictions on the generated rationales to satisfy certain constraints, e.g. compactness (Yu et al., 2019;Chalkidis et al., 2021). Note that such restrictions often result in lower performance and indeed are not guaranteed to explain the behaviour of an unconstrained model (Jain et al., 2020). The second type of approaches (the so called post hoc) usually rely on feature attribution methods, which assign an importance value to each input feature of a network (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Schulz et al., 2020). These methods do not allow for introducing useful biases during training, but focus on faithfully explaining model behaviour.

Feature attribution has a long tradition in image recognition tasks (Simonyan et al., 2013) and only recently have been applied to some NLP tasks, most commonly text classification (DeYoung et al., 2019). QE is fundamentally different from text classification where clues are typically separate words or phrases (Zaidan et al., 2007) which often can be considered independently of the rest of the text. This independence assumption does not hold for the task of evaluating translation quality where a word cannot be identified as a clue (e.g. translation error) without considering the surrounding context. Furthermore, SOTA NLP models based on contextualized representations for input words make rationale extraction especially challenging, as the representation for a given word can encode not only the word identity but also its interactions with other words in the text. Recent work has revealed various interesting properties that characterize the information flow through hidden layers in deep transformer models (Voita et al., 2019;De Cao et al., 2020;Yun et al., 2021). We provide additional insights on this topic in Section 5.2.
spans: Recent methods span both gradient-based and perturbation-based attributions in language models (Ancona et al., 2018;Bastings and Filippova, 2020). LIME and SHAP have also been adapted to NLP with token-level surrogates and additive explanations (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Lundberg and Lee, 2017). Integrated gradients have been benchmarked against leave-one-out for stability and faithfulness (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Hooker et al., 2019). A line of work studies causal mediation and attention as explanation signals (Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019;Vig et al., 2020).

document: Introduction Synthetic translations are widely leveraged in neural machine translation to expand training signals when genuine bitext is scarce. Early efforts explored monolingual exploitation through forward translation of source-side corpora (Zhang and Zong, 2016) and through backtranslation of target-side texts (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Marie et al., 2020). These strategies increased domain coverage and lexical diversity without costly human annotation. Subsequent work refined sampling, decoding, and filtering to control noise in generated pairs. Backtranslation quality has been tied to iterative retraining cycles and domain-adaptive language modeling. Data curation pipelines now routinely balance synthetic and human-authored segments for stability. Synthetic augmentation has also been used to target low-resource transfer by pivoting through related languages. Researchers study how noise temperature and sampling affect rare token recall. Some approaches interleave synthetic and authentic batches to mitigate distribution drift. Adaptive schedules adjust the ratio of synthetic sentences across training epochs. Nguyen et al. (2020) diversify the parallel data via translating both sides using multiple models and then merge them with the original to train a final NMT model; Jiao et al. (2020) employ a similar approach to rejuvenate inactive examples that contribute the least to the model performance. Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations. Distillation reframes the objective by training on model-generated hypotheses that smooth the target distribution. This perspective aligns with findings that simpler targets can reduce exposure bias during decoding. Non-autoregressive systems are a prominent case where distilled corpora stabilize optimization. Gu et al. (2017) report substantial speedups with competitive accuracy under distilled supervision. Later studies integrate iterative refinement and latent alignment to close quality gaps. Researchers analyze how teacher decoding strategies shape student generalization. Empirical evidence indicates that beam size and length penalties modulate calibration. Studies also examine the interaction between backtranslation and distillation in joint curricula. Mixed regimes alternate synthetic targets from different teachers for robustness. While its original goal was to guide the training of a student model of small capacity with the output of a teacher of high capacity, distillation is also necessary to effectively train some categories of MT architectures such as non-autoregressive models (Gu et al., 2017). While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021). Despite gains, concerns remain about domain narrowing and stylistic homogenization. Recent work proposes round-trip filtering to retain only meaning-preserving synthetic pairs. Others advocate uncertainty-aware selection to downweight brittle hypotheses. Evaluation protocols now emphasize robustness across domains and noise profiles. Our study situates these threads by comparing curriculum schedules that interleave backtranslation and distillation under fixed compute budgets. We focus on practical settings where monolingual data are abundant but parallel data are limited. The proposed analysis isolates the effect of teacher diversity, decoding temperature, and synthetic ratio. We report results on standard benchmarks spanning high- and low-resource pairs. Findings highlight when additional synthetic volume saturates and when diversity continues to help. We release code and preprocessing scripts to support reproducibility. Finally, we discuss open questions around measuring semantic adequacy in heavily distilled corpora.

(2020) employ a similar approach to rejuvenate inactive examples that contribute the least to the model performance. Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations.

While its original goal was to guide the training of a student model of small capacity with the output of a teacher of high capacity, distillation is also necessary to effectively train some categories of MT architectures such as non-autoregressive models (Gu et al., 2017). While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021).

Conversational question answering. In recent years, several conversational question answering datasets have emerged, such as QuAC (Choi et al., 2018), CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), andDoQA (Campos et al., 2020). Different from singleturn QA datasets (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), CQA requires the model to understand the question in the context of conversational history. There have been many methods proposed to improve CQA performance (Ohsugi et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020;Qu et al., 2019;Kim et al., 2021) and significant improvement has been made on CQA benchmarks.

Besides text-based CQA tasks, there also exist CQA benchmarks that require other forms of modeling ability, such as combining textual evidence with background knowledge (Saeidi et al., 2018), utilizing structured knowledge base (Saha et al., 2018;Guo et al., 2018), as well as CQA in other modalities (Das et al., 2017).

Evaluation with predicted history. Only recently has it been noticed that the current method of evaluating CQA models is flawed. Mandya et al. (2020); Siblini et al. (2021) point out that using gold answers in history is not consistent with the realworld scenario and propose to use predicted history for evaluation. Chen et al. (2020) analyze pretraining strategies for CQA encoders, and Qu et al. (2019) investigate dialog-level attention mechanisms for context tracking, while Kim et al. (2021) explore turn-level reformulation for error robustness. Different from prior work, in this paper, we conduct a large scale human evaluation to support our claims, identify the issues with predicted history, and propose rewriting questions to further mitigate the gap to human evaluation.
spans: Mandya et al. (2020); Siblini et al. (2021) point out that using gold answers in history is not consistent with the realworld scenario and propose to use predicted history for evaluation. Chen et al. (2020) analyze pretraining strategies for CQA encoders, and Qu et al. (2019) investigate dialog-level attention mechanisms for context tracking, while Kim et al. (2021) explore turn-level reformulation for error robustness.

Introduction

Natural language inference has catalyzed renewed interest in interpretability methods that connect model decisions to human-understandable evidence. Early studies argue that interpretability should be grounded in faithful attribution rather than post hoc plausibility (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Lipton, 2018). Subsequent work formalizes desiderata such as stability, sufficiency, and minimality for explanations tied to prediction changes (Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018; DeYoung et al., 2020). The community has converged on three major families of interpretability outputs relevant to inference tasks. Feature-based interpretation links internal activations to inputs through saliency, gradient-based scores, or perturbation analysis (Simonyan et al., 2013; Sundararajan et al., 2017; Jain and Wallace, 2019). Token-level interpretation highlights spans that are deemed crucial for the model decision, often trading off faithfulness and readability (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019). Sentence-level interpretation aims for human-readable rationales that summarize multi-sentence evidence into coherent justifications (Camburu et al., 2018; Narang et al., 2020). Despite progress, most approaches optimize only one objective—either prediction accuracy or explanation quality—without explicitly modeling their mutual reinforcement.

[Prior work on natural language inference often presupposes that extracted rationales directly support model predictions without clarifying how complementary evidence is reconciled across heterogeneous sources (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018). Camburu et al. (2018) study natural language explanations accompanying decisions. Talmor et al. (2018) examine multi-hop question answering over knowledge, and Kumar and Talukdar (2020) investigate graph-structured supervision for explainability.]

Within feature-based methods, attention maps are frequently used as surrogate explanations, yet their faithfulness remains contested due to weak correlation with gradient-based measures (Jain and Wallace, 2019; Serrano and Smith, 2019). Heatmap-style visualizations aggregate token importances but can obscure interactions across sentences, challenging their utility for multi-hop inference (Samek et al., 2017; Chefer et al., 2021). Gradient and integrated-gradient techniques offer sensitivity guarantees but may still highlight spurious correlations induced by dataset artifacts (Sundararajan et al., 2017; McCoy et al., 2019). Perturbation-based attribution improves faithfulness estimates at the cost of computational expense and potential distribution shift (Fong and Vedaldi, 2017; Hooker et al., 2019). These observations motivate modeling strategies that jointly calibrate predictions and explanations.

Token-level rationales have been operationalized through extractive selectors trained to preserve label fidelity while minimizing text length (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019). Selector–predictor frameworks disentangle rationale extraction from classification to improve faithfulness, but they risk overfitting to annotation heuristics (Chang et al., 2020). Human-annotated rationales provide supervision signals for selector quality, yet agreement varies across domains and tasks (DeYoung et al., 2020). Recent benchmarks in fact verification and question answering further highlight the gap between faithful span selection and end-task performance (Thorne et al., 2019; Lamm et al., 2021). Bridging this gap requires architectures that use rationales as intermediate computation rather than mere outputs.

Sentence-level explanations offer higher readability and can summarize dispersed evidence across documents (Camburu et al., 2018; Narang et al., 2020). Retrieval-augmented generators produce natural language justifications conditioned on external corpora, but separating factual grounding from stylistic fluency remains difficult (Lewis et al., 2020; Rashkin et al., 2021). Controllability mechanisms regulate explanation specificity and stance, yet explicit coupling with verifier modules is often absent (Kumar and Talukdar, 2020; Lampinen et al., 2022). Work on causal mediation suggests that explanations should serve as mediators aligning intermediate representations with downstream entailment (Feder et al., 2021). This perspective encourages training setups in which justifications actively influence the predictor’s internal reasoning trajectory.

From a learning-theoretic viewpoint, multi-task objectives can encourage shared representations that benefit both accuracy and explanation quality when tasks are related but not redundant (Ruder, 2017). Joint training with agreement constraints aligns selector salience with generator content, while calibration losses regularize uncertainty in both predictions and explanations (Desai and Durrett, 2020; Kamath et al., 2020). Curriculum strategies can stage learning from token-level to sentence-level rationales to progressively structure evidence (Bengio et al., 2009; Zhou et al., 2020). Such designs suggest a path for mutual promotion wherein better explanations produce better features and vice versa.

Our work formalizes mutual promotion as a closed-loop interaction between an inference module and a sentence-level explanation module. Concretely, the inference module proposes candidate evidence, the explanation module composes a justification, and a verifier adjudicates consistency between justification content and the predicted label. The resulting signals update both modules, rewarding alignments that improve task performance and penalizing unsupported claims. We also introduce a retrieval interface to supply verifiable facts that ground the generated explanations.

We evaluate on natural language inference and fact verification datasets where both labels and human-written justifications are available. Metrics include label accuracy, faithfulness via sufficiency and comprehensiveness tests, and explanation quality via factuality and entailment checks. Experimental results indicate that mutual promotion consistently improves both predictive performance and explanation grounding across domains. Ablations show that removing the verifier degrades faithfulness even when label accuracy remains competitive, underscoring the importance of closed-loop consistency. Qualitative analyses further illustrate how explanations evolve to cite more specific evidence as training progresses.

In summary, we position mutual promotion as a general strategy for coupling interpretability with predictive modeling in language understanding. By aligning the objectives of inference and explanation, our approach narrows the gap between plausibility and faithfulness. The framework is modular, data-efficient, and compatible with retrieval-augmented architectures. We release code and annotations to facilitate future work on controllable, verifiable explanations that improve decision quality.
spans: Prior work on natural language inference often presupposes that extracted rationales directly support model predictions without clarifying how complementary evidence is reconciled across heterogeneous sources (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018). Camburu et al. (2018) study natural language explanations accompanying decisions. Talmor et al. (2018) examine multi-hop question answering over knowledge, and Kumar and Talukdar (2020) investigate graph-structured supervision for explainability.

2.1 Security and Privacy Concern of Pre-trained Models

Pre-trained models like BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) rely heavily on the use of plain text data to get human-like performance. Despite the remarkable achievements of pre-trained models, these state-of-the-art models can not directly answer some sensitive use cases, including the medical record (Christoph et al., 2015), search history (Shen et al., 2007) and other personally identifiable information (PII).

To avoid the direct computation on plain-text data, recent works like TextHide (Huang et al., 2020) and DP-finetuning (Kerrigan et al., 2020) introduce the classical federated learning and differential privacy (DP) to protect the sensitive data. However, TextHide (Huang et al., 2020) can only be applied to sentence-level tasks. Due to the mixup operation, TextHide fails to model token-level tasks like named entity recognition or semantic role labelling. DP-finetuning would greatly sacrifice the performance of fine-tuned model by 20% perplexity for a generation model like GPT-2. Recent studies explore privacy-preserving objectives for pre-training such as PATE-style aggregation for language tasks (Papernot et al., 2018), cryptographic approaches for secure aggregation in federated optimization (Bonawitz et al., 2017), and model auditing via membership inference risk estimators that quantify exposure in generative models (Carlini et al., 2022). We focus on practical configurations that maintain utility while offering quantifiable privacy guarantees. Our analysis considers the trade-off between privacy budgets and downstream accuracy under realistic fine-tuning regimes. We compare task families that stress token-level labeling against sequence-level generation. Prior work highlights that token-level gradients tend to leak more information than sequence-level gradients. We examine privacy amplification effects arising from subsampling in large-batch training. Our setting includes common pre-training corpora with PII redaction strategies. We evaluate attack surfaces for text reconstruction during inference. We study the interaction between clipping norms and optimizer dynamics in DP-SGD. Our experiments include ablations on gradient noise schedules across epochs. We additionally report robustness under distribution shift in the presence of privacy noise. We provide guidance on choosing hyperparameters that balance utility and risk. Our findings suggest that moderate noise can preserve NER performance. We also observe task-dependent sensitivity to μ and σ in privacy accounting. These observations inform deployment recommendations for sensitive domains. We release reproducible pipelines for DP-aware fine-tuning. We further discuss compliance implications for healthcare and finance. Overall, our work clarifies practical privacy baselines for pre-trained models.
spans: Recent studies explore privacy-preserving objectives for pre-training such as PATE-style aggregation for language tasks (Papernot et al., 2018), cryptographic approaches for secure aggregation in federated optimization (Bonawitz et al., 2017), and model auditing via membership inference risk estimators that quantify exposure in generative models (Carlini et al., 2022).

document: Pretrained Language Models Contextual representations such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) are not just useful for monolingual representations. Multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019, mBERT), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), and XLM-RoBERTa (Conneau et al., 2020, XLM-R) have surprisingly high cross-lingual transfer performance compared to the previous best practice: static cross-lingual word embeddings (Pires et al., 2019;Wu and Dredze, 2019). Multilingual models are also practicalwhy have hundreds of separate models for each language when you could do better with just one? However, there's a catch. Conneau et al. (2020) reports the "curse of multilinguality": as the number of pretraining languages increases, the better the cross-lingual transfer is up to some point, after which the performance in zero-shot transfer starts to decrease. Our work complements Conneau et al. (2020) by explicitly dividing up between seen and unseen languages during pretraining and testing whether the "curse of multilinguality" is also an issue for unseen languages too. To ground our investigation, we consider standardized benchmarks in cross-lingual natural language understanding and structured prediction. We emphasize evaluation protocols that separate zero-shot transfer from in-language fine-tuning to avoid conflating adaptation with generalization. We further analyze scaling trends with respect to corpus size, language coverage, and tokenization granularity. Recent prompting-based alignment methods focus on steering model behavior without modifying parameters (Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023). Adapter-based transfer introduces lightweight modules to specialize shared backbones (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Task-balanced sampling and vocabulary sharing have been examined for data efficiency in multilingual pretraining (Chi et al., 2022). We build on these observations by isolating the impact of language exposure during pretraining while holding architecture and optimization constant. Our design contrasts a family of models with identical capacity but varying partitions of seen and unseen languages. We measure transfer both to typologically related and distant languages to probe whether overlap in subword statistics mediates the effect. We also ablate data temperature, per-language mixing ratios, and domain diversity to assess sensitivity to distributional skew. For robustness, we report results across multiple random seeds and training lengths to mitigate instability in low-resource regimes. We complement accuracy with calibration and consistency metrics to capture overconfidence and representational drift. To interpret transfer pathways, we analyze layer-wise probing results and lexical alignment scores derived from bilingual dictionaries. We include controlled ablations on tokenizer vocab size and share rate to disentangle segmentation from representation learning. We additionally evaluate catastrophic forgetting by fine-tuning on a subset of high-resource languages and re-testing zero-shot performance. Finally, we discuss practical implications for multilingual deployment, including model selection under compute budgets and the trade-off between language coverage and per-language performance.
spans: Recent prompting-based alignment methods focus on steering model behavior without modifying parameters (Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023). Adapter-based transfer introduces lightweight modules to specialize shared backbones (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Task-balanced sampling and vocabulary sharing have been examined for data efficiency in multilingual pretraining (Chi et al., 2022).

Our goal is to enable end-to-end training of goaldirected dialogue agents. In these settings, an agent aims to complete a particular task with its utterances (Smith and Hipp, 1994). Goal-directed agents have been explored in contexts such as personal assistants (McTear, 2002;Budzianowski et al., 2018;Williams et al., 2014), recommendation systems (Liu et al., 2010;Kang et al., 2019), education (Yuan et al., 2008), and negotiation (He et al., 2018;Lewis et al., 2017). While there are multiple approaches to constructing dialogue agents, in this work we frame the problem of generating dialogue as a sequential decision making problem within a (partially observed) Markov Decision Process (MDP) (Singh et al., 1999;Young et al., 2013). Prior works that utilize such an MDP formulation typically aim to train a dialogue management system (Singh et al., 2002), in which the agent reasons about higher-level abstractions of the state of the conversation, and language generation is performed using a downstream procedure. Dialogue management systems have been trained using techniques such as online reinforcement learning via policy gradients (Gašić et al., 2011;He et al., 2018), off-policy reinforcement learning (Pietquin et al., 2011;Yu et al., 2016) or actor-critic methods (Su et al., 2017). Our method differs from dialogue management systems in that CALM is an end-to-end system optimized for successful task completion, and performs both high-level decision making and language generation.

Recent advancements in language models, such as recurrent neural networks (Sundermeyer et al., 2012;Asri et al., 2016;Su et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhang et al., 2020) and attention-based architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2018;Brown et al., 2020), have spurred increasing interest in such endto-end dialogue systems (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Adiwardana et al., 2020). Modelbased approaches, in which a learned agent is substituted for a human, allow learning to be done entirely within simulation without human intervention (Li et al., 2016;He et al., 2018;Kang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018). In contrast to these approaches, CALM augments the traditional language modeling objective with taskspecific rewards in order to finetune a model that is more aware of task goals, which significantly improves performance over a naïve language model without the need for simulating human responses in an interactive training loop. Jaques et al. (2019) recently proposed a model-free, offline approach to undirected dialogue, or dialogue without a specific task goal. Our method differs in that we aim to solve goal-oriented dialogue which allows us to optimize task-specific objectives, and that we take a model-based RL approach which enables us to leverage fine-tuned language models.
spans: Recent advancements in language models, such as recurrent neural networks (Sundermeyer et al., 2012;Asri et al., 2016;Su et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhang et al., 2020) and attention-based architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2018;Brown et al., 2020), have spurred increasing interest in such endto-end dialogue systems (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Adiwardana et al., 2020). Modelbased approaches, in which a learned agent is substituted for a human, allow learning to be done entirely within simulation without human intervention (Li et al., 2016;He et al., 2018;Kang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018). In contrast to these approaches, CALM augments the traditional language modeling objective with taskspecific rewards in order to finetune a model that is more aware of task goals, which significantly improves performance over a naïve language model without the need for simulating human responses in an interactive training loop.

Automatic evaluation of open-domain dialogue systems has a multifaceted nature with many finegrained quality aspects (Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Turn-level aspects show the quality of the system's utterance given a dialogue context from different perspectives including appropriateness, relevance, engaging, and etc (Lowe et al., 2017;Tao et al., 2018;Ghazarian et al., 2020). Whereas, conversation-level facets such as coherence, diversity, informative take into account the whole conversation and dialog flow (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2021;Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Dialogue coherence evaluation is pertinent to the discourse coherence as a dialogue is counted as a multi-party discourse. Similar to discourse coherence, many original coherence evaluation metrics derived from Centering Model for monitoring the local focus of utterances and the entities distribution over utterances (Grosz and Sidner, 1986;Miltsakaki and Kukich, 2004;Lapata and Barzilay, 2005). A group of studies assess the coherence of dialogues with respect to entities and dialogue acts (Cervone and Riccardi, 2020;Mesgar et al., 2020). Another inspected approach for dialogue coherence evaluation is to represent dialogue in a structured graph format where contextually dependent neighbor utterances or concepts are connected nodes in the graph (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020). Graph convolutional networks are used to complete this task.

High quality training dataset is identified as one of the momentous and indelible components in automatic coherence evaluation. Some previous works construct such datasets by collecting human judgments (Higashinaka et al., 2014;Cervone and Riccardi, 2020). While many recent works rely on a more timely and costly affordable approach by automatically generating negative samples. The utterances of the coherent conversations from humanhuman interactions are manipulated by shuffling  their order, inserting or replacing irrelevant utterances (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In this work, we show that such changes can not truly represent machine generated incoherent conversations.
spans: Recent transformer-based metrics estimate dialog appropriateness via learned contextual representations (Zhang et al., 2021; Ghazarian et al., 2020). Lowe et al. (2017) formalize next-utterance retrieval benchmarks that correlate with human judgments. Tao et al. (2018) introduce context-aware hierarchical encoders for response evaluation. Vakulenko et al. (2018) explore knowledge graph structures for conversational search. Mehri and Eskénazi (2020) propose a multi-aspect evaluation suite that includes user-centric signals.

We review prior work on visual semantic AI, on the geometry and semantic properties of representations formed by language models, and on semantic intrinsic evaluation tasks.
Foundational studies in visual semantic AI combine recognition, grounding, and reasoning, advancing image–text alignment and relational queries with contrastive pretraining and compositional decoders (Frome et al., 2013; Antol et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2021).
Recent analyses of learned semantics in LMs examine probing-based structure recovery from hidden states (Hewitt and Manning, 2019), disentanglement of syntax and semantics (Tenney et al., 2019), typological and factual attribution (Ravfogel et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019), and clustering properties under linear maps (Ethayarajh, 2019). Liu et al. (2019) survey task sensitivity to fine-tuning and contextualization depth across GLUE. Brown et al. (2020) show scaling laws enable in-context induction of latent features in few-shot prompts. Bommasani et al. (2021) catalog emergent behaviors in foundation models across modalities. Geva et al. (2021) identify neurons acting as key–value memories that correlate with lexical facts.
On the geometric side, theoretical and empirical work characterizes anisotropy, representational collapse, and linear separability, linking curvature of embedding manifolds to retrieval and calibration (Mikolov et al., 2013; Mu and Viswanath, 2018; Gao et al., 2019; Aghajanyan et al., 2020).
Intrinsic semantic evaluation spans similarity, relatedness, and compositional generalization, with benchmarks such as SimLex-999, STS, and SCAN illustrating distinct validity and reliability profiles (Hill et al., 2015; Cer et al., 2017; Lake and Baroni, 2018).
spans: Recent analyses of learned semantics in LMs examine probing-based structure recovery from hidden states (Hewitt and Manning, 2019), disentanglement of syntax and semantics (Tenney et al., 2019), typological and factual attribution (Ravfogel et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019), and clustering properties under linear maps (Ethayarajh, 2019). Liu et al. (2019) survey task sensitivity to fine-tuning and contextualization depth across GLUE. Brown et al. (2020) show scaling laws enable in-context induction of latent features in few-shot prompts. Bommasani et al. (2021) catalog emergent behaviors in foundation models across modalities. Geva et al. (2021) identify neurons acting as key–value memories that correlate with lexical facts.

In this section, we will briefly introduce the background of adaptive kNN-MT (Zheng et al., 2021a). Adaptive kNN-MT is derived from kNN-MT (Khandelwal et al., 2021) by inserting a lightweight Meta-k Network that fuses kNN retrievals with various k to alleviate the possible noise induced by a single k. Recent work on retrieval-augmented translation has examined cache-based adaptation (Kuang et al., 2018), lexical-constrained decoding strategies (Post and Vilar, 2018), and memory-augmented transformers for domain shift (Bapna and Firat, 2019). Studies on bilingual lexicon induction report complementary benefits from nearest-neighbor search in embedding spaces (Artetxe et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2018). Investigations into example-based machine translation predate neural approaches and document phrase memory effects (Koehn and Senellart, 2010). Formally, it is formulated as two steps: target-side datastore creation and Metak Network predictions. Target-side Datastore Creation. The datastore constists of a set of key-value pairs. Given a bilingual sentence pair (s, t) in a corpus (S, T ), a pretrained general domain NMT model autoregressively extracts the context representation h i of the i-th target token conditioned on both source and target context (s, t <i ), denoted as

The datastore is finally constructed by taking h i as keys and t i as values:

Meta-k Network Prediction. Meta-k Network (f β ) is a two-layer feed-forward network followed by a non-linear activation function. Based on the constructed datastore, it considers a set of various ks that are smaller than an upper bound K, the standard setting is k ∈ Q where Q = {0} ∪ {2 j |j ∈ N, 2 j ≤ K}. K nearest neighbors of the current context query ĥi from the datastore are first retrieved at the i-th decoding step. Then the l 2 distance from ĥi to each neighbor (h j , v j ) is denoted as d i = h j , ĥi 2 . And the count of distinct values in top j neighbors are denoted as c j . The normalized weights of each available k are obtained:

, where f β denotes the Meta-k Network. The ultimate prediction probability is ensembled:

Note that a validation set is usually required to study the Meta-k Network before predicting on test

Studies on bilingual lexicon induction report complementary benefits from nearest-neighbor search in embedding spaces (Artetxe et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2018). Investigations into example-based machine translation predate neural approaches and document phrase memory effects (Koehn and Senellart, 2010).
