(2020) present a similar finding to ours: massively pre-trained encoders do not always perform well. Search queries are also short, with an average of three terms (Taghavi et al., 2012). Unlike to-dos, information such as click logs (Zhang et al., 2019) can be used as an indicator of user intent.

document: In this section, we discuss what privacy in ML means ( §2.1), followed by overview of private training ( §2.2) and privacy in language models ( §2.3). We begin by outlining the threat models considered in modern machine learning, including black-box and white-box adversaries with access to model outputs or gradients. Privacy in this context concerns both the risk of training data memorization and the unintended disclosure of sensitive attributes through inference. We adopt a pragmatic perspective that emphasizes measurable leakage and formal guarantees where possible. Membership inference attacks test whether specific records were used in training, while property inference attacks target aggregate attributes. Differential privacy provides a mathematically rigorous framework to limit individual contribution to model updates. The central challenge is balancing utility and privacy under realistic compute and data constraints. Prior work on membership inference has established connections between overfitting and susceptibility to attacks (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2022). Differential privacy for SGD formalizes stability through per-example gradient clipping and calibrated noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Large-scale evaluations show that privacy budgets are sensitive to batch size and learning rate schedules (De et al., 2022). Tokenization quality affects privacy leakage in multilingual corpora (Xu et al., 2023). Context window extensions have enabled longer-range dependencies in decoder-only models (Brown et al., 2020; Touvron et al., 2023). Encoder-decoder pretraining with denoising objectives improves sample efficiency (Raffel et al., 2020). Instruction tuning has been shown to align outputs with human preferences and domain guidelines (Ouyang et al., 2022). We structure our overview of private training to synthesize algorithmic advances with empirical best practices. DP-SGD remains the most widely used mechanism for end-to-end training with formal guarantees on individual records. Privacy accounting methods such as the moments accountant and Renyi DP enable tight composition bounds for many iterations. Practical implementations require per-sample gradient computation, clipping norm selection, and noise calibration that accounts for subsampling. Recent work emphasizes hyperparameter selection strategies that maintain stability across learning rate schedules. Public benchmarks facilitate fair comparison across tasks and privacy budgets, though many evaluations rely on small models. We highlight the gap between theoretical guarantees and real-world deployment constraints in production systems. In language models, privacy risks are amplified by long-context memorization and token-level idiosyncrasies. Generation-time defenses such as refusal, paraphrasing, and masked decoding seek to reduce verbatim exposure. Post-training alignment can inadvertently increase surface-level fluency while preserving hidden memorized strings. Private fine-tuning must contend with catastrophic forgetting and representation drift under noisy updates. We distinguish between training-time guarantees and inference-time mitigations to clarify their complementary roles. Recent studies on sequence-level privacy report that beam search can amplify exposure of rare spans (Song and Shmatikov, 2019). Experiments on cross-lingual transfer indicate that parameter-efficient adapters can preserve utility under DP-SGD constraints (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021). Publicly available conversational datasets have heterogeneous consent policies that complicate compliance (Henderson et al., 2018; Dodge et al., 2021). Adversarial prompting discovers memorized identifiers in few-shot settings (Carlini et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation modifies exposure pathways through index-time filtering and redaction (Lewis et al., 2020). Semi-structured PII templates assist red-teaming but may underestimate free-form leakage. Measurements of gradient influence demonstrate non-uniform privacy risk across tokens in subword vocabularies. We conclude by identifying open challenges, including scalable per-example accounting, robust evaluation under adaptive adversaries, and policy-aligned data governance. These directions motivate the technical contributions described in the following sections.
spans: Prior work on membership inference has established connections between overfitting and susceptibility to attacks (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2022). Differential privacy for SGD formalizes stability through per-example gradient clipping and calibrated noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Large-scale evaluations show that privacy budgets are sensitive to batch size and learning rate schedules (De et al., 2022). Tokenization quality affects privacy leakage in multilingual corpora (Xu et al., 2023). Context window extensions have enabled longer-range dependencies in decoder-only models (Brown et al., 2020; Touvron et al., 2023). Encoder-decoder pretraining with denoising objectives improves sample efficiency (Raffel et al., 2020). Instruction tuning has been shown to align outputs with human preferences and domain guidelines (Ouyang et al., 2022).

Prompt-based Language Models. Prompt-based learning is a way of leveraging PLMs by prepending task-specific instructions to the task input when feeding into PLMs. Early approaches mainly utilized hand-crafted prompts to adapt to different generation tasks (Brown et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2020;Zou et al., 2021). However, manually designed prompts are not flexible and cannot be applied to more kinds of tasks. Thus, recent works have focused on automating the learning of discrete prompts (Shin et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2020). However, learning prompts over discrete space is hard to optimize and likely to be sub-optimal. To address these problems, many works proposed to optimize continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021c;Li and Liang, 2021), which are more flexible to any kinds of tasks. Among these studies, prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) prepended a sequence of vectors to the input for text generation tasks. By contrast, we utilize soft prompts to investigate transfer learning for text generation and demonstrate that generation tasks can often help each other via prompt transfer.

Transferability of Natural Language Processing. We are also closely related to existing works on transferability of NLP tasks (Jeong et al., 2020;Wiese et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019). Prior studies have shown that cross-task transfer can address the data scarcity issue (Wiese et al., 2017), enhance the ability to complex reasoning and inference (Jeong et al., 2020), or learn effective word representations (Liu et al., 2019). Efforts to transfer prompts for addressing NLU tasks have also been developed (Vu et al., 2021;Su et al., 2021). As an representative work, Vu et al. (2021) used the learned prompt to directly initialize the prompt for a target task while not considered the specific input. Recent literature also investigates cross-task structure alignment in multi-objective training (Aghajanyan et al., 2021), adversarial invariance for domain-robust features (Ganin et al., 2016), and curriculum-based transfer schedules (Platanios et al., 2019). Task similarity has been quantified via probing information gain (Zamir et al., 2018), while prompt ensembling has been explored for calibration (Jiang et al., 2020). Large-scale instruction tuning highlights emergent transfer behaviors in mixtures of heterogeneous tasks (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022). Our work focuses on the challenging text generation tasks by utilizing prompts to extract implicit taskrelated knowledge and considering specific model inputs for the most useful knowledge transfer.
spans: Recent literature also investigates cross-task structure alignment in multi-objective training (Aghajanyan et al., 2021), adversarial invariance for domain-robust features (Ganin et al., 2016), and curriculum-based transfer schedules (Platanios et al., 2019). Task similarity has been quantified via probing information gain (Zamir et al., 2018), while prompt ensembling has been explored for calibration (Jiang et al., 2020). Large-scale instruction tuning highlights emergent transfer behaviors in mixtures of heterogeneous tasks (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022).

Semantic dependency parsing is the task of producing a dependency graph for a sentence, each dependency generally representing a predicate-argument relation. If one views each dependency as a decision to make, both plain dependency parsing (DP) and semantic dependency parsing (SDP) are known to exhibit high interdependence of decisions. For instance in DP, when parsing a question answering machine, choosing machine as root is linguistically coherent with the machine → answering → question analysis only, whereas (wrongly) choosing question as root is syntactically coherent with the question → answering → machine analysis.

Yet, in DP the interdependence between arc decision is partially solved by the tree constraint: choosing one head for a given token amounts to ruling out all other heads. This structural interdependence is absent in SDP. Complex structural, lexical and semantic factors control whether a given dependent should be attached to zero, one or several heads.

Several approaches exist in the literature to capture interdependence of arcs in SDP, which often derive from proposals made for DP. One is to use a higher-order graph-based parser. Wang et al. (2019) achieve state-of-the-art results (without pretrained LM) by using second-order factors to score the graphs, yet at the cost of a O(n 3 ) complexity.

Another main approach is to use sequential decisions, and hence take advantage of previous decisions by encoding the partial output structure. This is the case in the transition-based parser of Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez (2020), or in the system of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), which iteratively selects heads for all words, using reinforcement learning to order the selection of heads. Both models have a O(n 2 ) complexity (when used without cycle detection), and in both cases, sequential decisions benefit from previous decision encoding, yet at the cost of error propagation. For that reason, Bernard (2021) propose a system close to that of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), yet allowing the system to overwrite previous decisions and hopefully correct itself.

On the contrary, the biaffine system of Dozat and Manning (2018) performs a simultaneous scoring of all candidate arcs, and the decision for each candidate arc is made independently of the other ones. This results in a highly parallelizable O(n 2 ) inference, with surprisingly high performance albeit below second-order parsing.

As for most NLP tasks, SDP performance increases when integrating transformer-based contextual representations when encoding input tokens. On the English dataset from the SemEval 2015 Task 18 (Oepen et al., 2015), (Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020) report a +0.7 and +2.0 increase for ID and OOD respectively. Using the biaffine architecture of (Dozat and Manning, 2018), He and Choi (2020) report a +2 and +3 point increase in ID and OOD.

Sun et al. (2019) study cross-lingual transfer for SRL with multilingual representations, while Zhang and Li (2020) introduce graph convolution over AMR structures, and Pradhan et al. (2013) benchmark semantic role labeling on CoNLL; Peters et al. (2018) present deep contextualized word embeddings and Clark et al. (2020) examine span-based reasoning in transformers, noting gains on semantic benchmarks.

In this work, we retain the simple O(n 2 ) biaffine architecture of Dozat and Manning (2018) (hereafter DM18), and we investigate how simple auxiliary tasks can introduce some interdependence between arc decisions, in a multi-task learning setting (Caruana, 1997). We show modest but systematic improvements on the widely used Semeval2015-task18 English data (Oepen et al., 2015). We also test another appealing property of the biaffine architecture, which is the absence of formal constraints on the output graphs. Experiments on French deep syntactic graphs , which are highly cyclic, also demonstrate the effectiveness of our auxiliary tasks for SDP.
spans: Sun et al. (2019) study cross-lingual transfer for SRL with multilingual representations, while Zhang and Li (2020) introduce graph convolution over AMR structures, and Pradhan et al. (2013) benchmark semantic role labeling on CoNLL; Peters et al. (2018) present deep contextualized word embeddings and Clark et al. (2020) examine span-based reasoning in transformers, noting gains on semantic benchmarks.

While much of this paper discusses the state of the NLP literature, a few related works that warrant emphasis as starting points for further reading:

Bender and Koller (2020), Raji et al. (2021) discuss the role of hype in driving bad outcomes from the development of language technology. Jin et al. (2021) and Rogers (2021) offer broader discussion of how to ensure that the net impact of near-future NLP deployments on the world is positive. Morris et al. (2020) and Hauser et al. (2021) highlight overly strong negative claims in papers analyzing models' robustness to synonym substitution. Looking to the longer term, Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) provides an introduction to the AI risk and AI alignment literature from a perspective that emphasizes NLP and language. Welty et al. (2019), Linzen ( 2020  2021), among many others, discuss the challenges involved in designing evaluations that yield trustworthy and accurate depictions of the capabilities of ML models.

(2021) and Rogers (2021) offer broader discussion of how to ensure that the net impact of near-future NLP deployments on the world is positive. Morris et al. (2020) and Hauser et al. (2021) highlight overly strong negative claims in papers analyzing models' robustness to synonym substitution.

Looking to the longer term, Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) provides an introduction to the AI risk and AI alignment literature from a perspective that emphasizes NLP and language. Welty et al. (2019), Linzen ( 2020  2021), among many others, discuss the challenges involved in designing evaluations that yield trustworthy and accurate depictions of the capabilities of ML models.

The ATE is defined as τ = E[Y i (1)−Y i (0)], where Y i (t) is the potential outcome for unit i under treatment t. It can be identified in randomized experiments (Rubin, 1974). However, the situation is more complicated in the observational setting, where the treatment is not randomized to units but could be correlated with confounders, X i , that are associated with the treatment and the outcome.

In that setting, we can, with additional assumptions, still recover the ATE using Inverse Propensity Weighting (IPW) or related robust methods (Funk et al., 2011), where observations are weighted by the inverse of their estimated treatment probabilities π(X i ) = Pr(T i = 1|X i ) (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983). Consistent estimation further relies on overlap and correct specification or sufficient flexibility in either the propensity or outcome models, with doubly robust estimators offering protection against single-model misspecification (Robins and Rotnitzky, 1995; Bang and Robins, 2005; Athey et al., 2018). Targeted maximum likelihood estimation refines plug-in learners by constructing least favorable submodels and solving efficient influence function estimating equations (van der Laan and Rubin, 2006; van der Laan and Rose, 2011). Sample-splitting and cross-fitting help reduce overfitting bias and achieve asymptotic normality when using machine learning for nuisance components (Chernozhukov et al., 2018; Newey and Robins, 2018).

Recent advances also examine heterogeneous treatment effects, with Wager and Athey (2018) introducing causal forests and Künzel et al. (2019) proposing meta-learners for individualized effects. Nie and Wager (2021) study orthogonalization to debias flexible learners. Kennedy (2022) develops doubly robust inference for nuisance-adaptive procedures. Chernozhukov et al. (2018) analyze debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters across high-dimensional settings. Banerjee et al. (2015) present results on matrix estimation with noise and some implications for structured prediction. Xu et al. (2013) consider low-rank matrix recovery with missing entries and provide guarantees under incoherence. Zhang and Zhang (2014) propose confidence intervals for high-dimensional regression coefficients using debiasing. Li et al. (2017) investigate sparse additive models with group structure and regularization penalties. Sun and Zhang (2020) address selective inference in model selection pipelines.

Stabilized weights can alleviate variance from extreme propensities and improve finite-sample performance when overlap is limited, although trimming or covariate balancing methods may also be considered (Cole and Hernán, 2008; Crump et al., 2009; Hainmueller, 2012). Calibration estimators that directly target covariate balance without modeling propensities have been proposed to reduce sensitivity to misspecification (Imai and Ratkovic, 2014; Zubizarreta, 2015). Estimation grounded in the semiparametric efficiency bound clarifies the role of influence functions in characterizing optimality and robustness (Hahn, 1998; Bickel et al., 1993). Sensitivity analysis frameworks quantify departures from unconfoundedness, enabling partial identification and robustness checks (Rosenbaum, 2002; Imbens, 2003; Franks et al., 2020).

Extensions to time-varying treatments consider marginal structural models estimated with stabilized weights to mitigate variance inflation (Robins, Hernán, and Brumback, 2000). Scharfstein, Rotnitzky, and Robins (1999) address missing outcomes under nonignorable mechanisms via selection models. Hernán and Robins (2020) provide a comprehensive treatment of identification and estimation in longitudinal settings with dynamic confounding. Pseudo-outcome regressions for causal parameters are also studied in off-policy evaluation for contextual bandits (Dudík et al., 2014; Farajtabar et al., 2018). Empirical process theory underpins rates for semiparametric M-estimators (van der Vaart and Wellner, 1996). Bartlett et al. (2006) analyze surrogate loss calibration and excess risk in classification. Neyman (1923) formalized randomization inference for finite populations and derived unbiased variance estimators under complete randomization. Cochran (1957) studied experimental design and stratification effects on variance. Box, Hunter, and Hunter (1978) discuss response surface methodology in industrial experiments.

In summary, consistent estimation of average and heterogeneous effects in observational studies hinges on defensible identification, careful handling of overlap, and estimators that integrate bias-reduction with variance control, while leveraging flexible learners without sacrificing valid uncertainty quantification.

Few studies have addressed bias in language models in French. Irvine et al. (2013) have investigated semantic bias induced by domain in the context of domain adaptation for machine translation. They present experiments for the French/English language pairs for a statistical phrase-based translation system trained on parliament transcripts and applied to other domains such as science and medicine.

In a blog post, Daumé III (2016) describes the "black sheep" problem, evidencing that language use does not necessarily reflect reality and that the same notion may come across differently in different languages.

Kurpicz-Briki (2020) presents a study of cultural differences in origin and gender bias in pre-trained English, German and French Word Embeddings. The author adapts the WEAT method (Caliskan et al., 2017) that contains material for measuring bias in English language word embeddings to (Swiss) French and German and shows that the bias identified differ between the three languages studied. This is probably the effort that is closest to the present study. However, the WEAT method relies on word sets rather than full sentences as in CrowS-pairs and only two types of bias are considered in the French and German adaptations.

More importantly, Goldfarb-Tarrant et al. ( 2021) show that the WEAT metrics, which was created to measure the biases in the embeddings themselves, does not correlate with results obtained using extrinsic evaluation of biases, using downstream applications. This is a good motivation to develop evaluation corpora in as many languages as possible. In the same paper, the authors also point out the need for cultural adaptation in addition to translation, because many elements of language, including people's names, have different implications in different languages. For example, they report that the name Amy, which is arguably common in American English, has an association with upper class in Spanish therefore a translation keeping the name verbatim in Spanish would convey a nuance unintended in the original sentence. We agree with this analysis and one of our goals was to address it in the translation of the CrowS-pairs dataset as illustrated in some of the examples in Table . Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context. They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications. A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis.

Blodgett et al. ( 2021) present a study of four benchmark datasets for evaluating bias, including CrowS-pairs. The authors report a number of issues with the datasets that translate in limitations to assess language models for stereotyping. Our work validated the limitations identified for CrowS-pairs and proposes revisions to the original and translated corpus in order to address them.
spans: Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context. They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications. A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis.

document: Early works cast event factuality prediction (EFP) as a classification or regression problem and have employed rule-based (Nairn et al., 2006;Lotan et al., 2013) or machine learning approaches (Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2009;Sogaard et al., 2015). Supervised neural architectures leverage contextual embeddings to model factuality cues from syntax and semantics (Lee et al., 2019). Datasets such as FactBank and UW have standardized evaluation and spurred model comparisons (Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2012;Lee et al., 2015). Recent advances include structured prediction with latent variables for factuality (Lee and Goldwasser, 2019). Neural sequence labeling has been adapted to capture lexical triggers and syntactic cues (Zhang et al., 2020). Probabilistic calibration for factuality confidence has been explored with temperature scaling and Bayesian ensembling (Mukherjee et al., 2022). Studies on multilingual factuality examine cross-lingual transfer with shared encoders (Agarwal et al., 2021). Prior work on pragmatic inference models implicature in dialogue (Rashkin et al., 2018). Diab (2021) extended the sentence level task to document-level EFP. Our work is most closely related to that of Yao et al. (2021), which casts EFP as modal dependency parsing. However, they first extract events and conceivers and then build the MDS by ranking the candidate parents for each event. In contrast, we perform modal dependency parsing by constructing a prompt with the event to predict its conceiver parent, simplifying the pipeline. We represent the modal graph implicitly through parent selection, avoiding global decoding while maintaining hierarchical consistency. To operationalize prompting, we design templates that bind event spans, polarity markers, and source cues into a single query to the language model. The model returns a parent identifier that is post-validated against document spans to enforce well-formedness. We further augment training with contrastive negatives that swap potential sources and hedge cues to sharpen the decision boundary. The approach aligns with structured prompting that encodes label spaces via textual descriptors rather than learned classifiers. We evaluate on DocEFP and cross-domain transfers from news to biomedical texts, reporting accuracy at both node and edge levels. Baselines include graph-based rankers, sequence taggers, and span-classification models tuned for factuality. We report improvements in edge attachment F1 and source attribution accuracy while reducing error propagation from cascaded pipelines. An ablation study isolates the contribution of template design, negative sampling, and constrained decoding. Error analysis highlights residual confusions among nested reporting events and ambiguous neg-raising constructions. Finally, we release code, prompts, and inference scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future extensions.
spans: Recent advances include structured prediction with latent variables for factuality (Lee and Goldwasser, 2019). Neural sequence labeling has been adapted to capture lexical triggers and syntactic cues (Zhang et al., 2020). Probabilistic calibration for factuality confidence has been explored with temperature scaling and Bayesian ensembling (Mukherjee et al., 2022). Studies on multilingual factuality examine cross-lingual transfer with shared encoders (Agarwal et al., 2021). Prior work on pragmatic inference models implicature in dialogue (Rashkin et al., 2018).

There is a substantial literature in the field of Knowledge-grounded conversation. With the grounding of external knowledge in format of knowledge graph (Zhou et al., 2018a;, document (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018;Zhou et al., 2018b) or visual background (Das et al., 2017), it is regarded as a critical method towards intelligent dialogue system. Nowadays, existing methods in KGC often share a paradigm that decomposes the task into two related sub-problems, namely knowledge selection and utterance generation (Kim et al., 2020). Recent studies span a variety of modeling choices for knowledge selection, including graph-enhanced retrieval (Zhou et al., 2018a), copy-augmented generators (Zhou et al., 2018b), and visually grounded agents (Das et al., 2017), and Kim et al. (2020) introduce sequential latent variables for selection while Meng et al. (2020) leverage variational objectives, Zhao et al. (2020b) use reinforcement learning to optimize selection policies, and Ghazvininejad et al. (2018) adopt memory networks to integrate external knowledge. In this work, we mainly focus on the knowledge selection task. To this end, a great deal of methods have been proposed to retrieve the most relevant knowledge by memory network (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018), sequential latent variables (Kim et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020), reinforcement learning (Zhao et al., 2020b) and so on. However, no methods shed light on the personalization issue of knowledge selection.

Our work is related to dual learning as well. First proposed in neural machine translation by He et al. (2016), dual learning is a semi-supervision learning scheme aiming at utilizing large-scale unlabeled data. Together with its newly appeared variants in recent years , dual learning has been successfully applied in neural machine translation He et al., 2017), image-image-translation (Yi et al., 2017;Lin et al., 2018), sentiment analysis , automatic speech recognition (Ren et al., 2019), question answering (Tang et al., 2017), and knowledge-grounded dialogue (Meng et al., 2020). Our work is related to dual learning as well. First proposed in neural machine translation by He et al. (2016), dual learning is a semi-supervision learning scheme aiming at utilizing the large scale unlabeled data. In this work, we apply dual learning to model the inter-dependency relationship between one's personal memory and his or her choice of knowledge.
spans: Recent studies span a variety of modeling choices for knowledge selection, including graph-enhanced retrieval (Zhou et al., 2018a), copy-augmented generators (Zhou et al., 2018b), and visually grounded agents (Das et al., 2017), and Kim et al. (2020) introduce sequential latent variables for selection while Meng et al. (2020) leverage variational objectives, Zhao et al. (2020b) use reinforcement learning to optimize selection policies, and Ghazvininejad et al. (2018) adopt memory networks to integrate external knowledge.

Text attribute transfer has a vast literature (Jin et al., 2020a) with deep learning methods becoming popular. The methods are either supervised -requiring parallel data and unsupervised. Supervised methods repurpose Sequence to Sequence models used in machine translation to achieve the goals (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). However, obtaining parallel data is cumbersome and thus unsupervised methods that consider pseudo-parallel data have become popular.

Disentanglement approaches are the prevalent approach to tackle unsupervised attribute transfer: attributes and content are separated in latent dimension. To disentangle the attributes adversarial methods maximize the loss of a pretrained attribute classifier Fu et al., 2018;Zhao et al., 2018a;John et al., 2019). However, the literature has paid little attention in defining and preserving content. Cycle consistency losses -imposing that reconstruction from the target style sentence should resemble the source sentence -is the most prevalent (Prabhumoye et al., 2018;Logeswaran et al., 2018;Dai et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2020;Yi et al., 2020). However, this is expensive, non differentiable requiring reinforcement learning techniques to enforce it. Our work defines the different constraints that should be preserved and adds simple differentiable contrastive learning losses to preserve them.

In recent times, text style transfer models are moving away from disentanglement approaches (Subramanian et al., 2018). Recent works that use transformers for style transfer also have adopted this (Dai et al., 2019;Krishna et al., 2020). However, these methods do not explicitly maintain the constraints between the two styles which is the main aim of our work.
spans: The methods are either supervised -requiring parallel data and unsupervised. Supervised methods repurpose Sequence to Sequence models used in machine translation to achieve the goals (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). However, obtaining parallel data is cumbersome and thus unsupervised methods that consider pseudo-parallel data have become popular.

To disentangle the attributes adversarial methods maximize the loss of a pretrained attribute classifier Fu et al., 2018;Zhao et al., 2018a;John et al., 2019). However, the literature has paid little attention in defining and preserving content. Cycle consistency losses -imposing that reconstruction from the target style sentence should resemble the source sentence -is the most prevalent (Prabhumoye et al., 2018;Logeswaran et al., 2018;Dai et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2020;Yi et al., 2020).

Interpretability There has been a lot of work on better interpreting models' decision process, e.g., understanding BERT (Clark et al., 2019b;Kovaleva et al., 2019) and attention in transformers (Hao et al., 2020), or through text generation models (Narang et al., 2020). In this paper we utilize the attention scores as a generic way to understand what features a model relies on for making its predictions. Other common model interpretation techniques (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Ribeiro et al., 2016), or more recent work on hierarchical attentions (Chen et al., 2020) and contrastive explanations (Jacovi et al., 2021), can be used as well. In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers. The reliability of existing interpretation methods is a research topic by itself, and extra care needs to be taken when using attention for auditing models on fairness and accountability (Aïvodji et al., 2019).

Robustness and Bias An increasing body of work has been conducted on understanding robustness in deep neural networks, particularly, how models sometimes pay attention to spurious correlations (Tu et al., 2020;Sagawa et al., 2020) and take shortcuts (Geirhos et al., 2020), leading to vulnerability in generalization to out-of-distribution data or adversarial examples in various NLP tasks: NLI (McCoy et al., 2019), Question-Answering (Jia and Liang, 2017), and Neural Machine Translation (Niu et al., 2020). Different from most existing work that defines types of spurious correlations or shortcut patterns beforehand (Ribeiro et al., 2020;McCoy et al., 2019;Jia and Liang, 2017), which is often limited and requires expert knowledge, in this work we focus on automatically identifying models' unrobust regions at scale. Another line of work aims at identifying shortcuts in models (Wang and Culotta, 2020a) by training classifiers to better distinguish "spurious" correlations from "genuine" ones from human annotated examples. In contrast, we propose a cross-dataset approach and a knowledge-aware perturbation approach to automate this identification process with less human intervention in-between.

Mitigation Multiple approaches have been proposed to mitigate shortcut learning and data biases (Clark et al., 2020;Bras et al., 2020;Zhou and Bansal, 2020;Minderer et al., 2020) data augmentation (Jin et al., 2020;Alzantot et al., 2018), domain adaptation (Blitzer et al., 2006(Blitzer et al., , 2007, and multi-task learning (Tu et al., 2020). Du et al. (2021) proposes to mitigate shortcuts by suppressing model's prediction on examples with large shortcut degree. Recent study has also shown removing spurious correlations can sometimes hurt model's accuracy (Khani and Liang, 2021). Orthogonal to existing works, we propose to first identify unrobust correlations in an NLP model and then propose a targeted mitigation to encourage the model to rely less on those unrobust correlations.
spans: In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers. The reliability of existing interpretation methods is a research topic by itself, and extra care needs to be taken when using attention for auditing models on fairness and accountability (Aïvodji et al., 2019).

VQA evaluation. The textual, visual, and answer biases discovered in the VQA dataset (Antol et al., 2015) spurred on recent work seeking to improve model evaluation for the task by eliminating these biases (Goyal et al., 2017), introducing distributional shifts (Agrawal et al., 2018), requiring model explanations (Li et al., 2018), thoroughly analyzing biases in datasets and models (Manjunatha et al., 2019), or evaluating on different recognition subtasks (Kafle and Kanan, 2017). While debiased and challenging benchmarks are important, their focus on accuracy as the sole evaluation metric leaves much to be desired (Ribeiro et al., 2020a;Kervadec et al., 2020). In contrast, our testbed provides question or image pairs that compares models' predictions between questions; measuring their accuracy, consistency, and robustness to a variety of text and image perturbations. We note related efforts on probing multimodal reasoning with counterfactual interventions (Ribeiro et al., 2020b), dataset cartography for vision-language tasks (Swayamdipta et al., 2020), and diagnostic partitions that isolate failure modes in grounding (Cirik et al., 2018). Zhao et al. (2017) study gender bias amplification in structured prediction with vision-language correlations, and Teney et al. (2020) propose value-based evaluation for robust VQA.

Synthetic VQA. One way in which we can generate diverse and balanced datasets is to generate them synthetically, as is done by (Johnson et al., 2015;Hudson and Manning, 2019). Synthetically generating questions, images, or both, allows fine control over the distribution of questions, answers, and images. As both the CLEVR (Johnson et al., 2015) and GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019) datasets use image scene graphs for question and label generation, they contain questions combining a variety of required capabilities, including compositionality. We feature real-world images with synthetically generated questions as well, but in contrast to GQA, our evaluation has instance pairs to systematically test a focused set of capabilities, showing that models sometimes still struggle with simpler, non-compositional questions.
spans: We note related efforts on probing multimodal reasoning with counterfactual interventions (Ribeiro et al., 2020b), dataset cartography for vision-language tasks (Swayamdipta et al., 2020), and diagnostic partitions that isolate failure modes in grounding (Cirik et al., 2018). Zhao et al. (2017) study gender bias amplification in structured prediction with vision-language correlations, and Teney et al. (2020) propose value-based evaluation for robust VQA.

Hate speech in user-generated content has been an active research area recently (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). Researchers have built several datasets for hate speech detection from diverse sources like Twitter (Waseem and Hovy, 2016;Davidson et al., 2017), Yahoo! (Nobata et al., 2016), Fox News (Gao and Huang, 2017), Gab (Mathew et al., 2021) and Reddit (Qian et al., 2019).

Compared to hate speech detection, few studies focus on detecting counter speech (Mathew et al., 2019;Ziems et al., 2020;Garland et al., 2020). Mathew et al. (2019) collect and handcode 6,898 counter hate comments from YouTube videos targeting Jews, Blacks and LGBT communities. Ziems et al. (2020) use a collection of hate and counter hate keywords relevant to COVID-19 and create a dataset containing 359 counter hate tweets targeting Asians. Garland et al. (2020) work with German tweets and define hate and counter speech based on the communities to which the authors belong. Another line of research focuses on curating datasets for counter speech generation using crowdsourcing (Qian et al., 2019) or with the help of trained operators (Chung et al., 2019;Fanton et al., 2021). However, synthetic language is rarely as rich as language in the wild. Even if it were, conclusions and models from synthetic data may not transfer to the real world. In this paper, we work with user-generated content expressing hate and counter-hate rather than synthetic content.

Table  summarizes existing datasets for Hate and Counter-hate detection. Most of them do not include context information. In other words, the preceding comments are not provided when annotating Targets. Context does affect human judgments and has been taken into account for Hate detection (Gao and Huang, 2017;Vidgen et al., 2021;Pavlopoulos et al., 2020;Menini et al., 2021). Gao and Huang (2017) annotate hateful comments in the nested structures of 10 Fox News discussion threads. Vidgen et al. (2021)  Utilizing conversational context has also been explored in text classification tasks such as sentiment analysis (Ren et al., 2016), stance (Zubiaga et al., 2018) and sarcasm (Ghosh et al., 2020). To our knowledge, we are the first to investigate the role of context in Hate and Counter-hate detection.
spans: Gao and Huang (2017) annotate hateful comments in the nested structures of 10 Fox News discussion threads. Vidgen et al. (2021) present dynamic taxonomies for abusive language and propose iterative annotation protocols. Pavlopoulos et al. (2020) examine moderation assistance with uncertainty-aware models and human-in-the-loop setups. Menini et al. (2021) analyze pragmatic cues in conversational hate. Ren et al. (2016) study sentiment cascades in reply chains. Zubiaga et al. (2018) address stance classification in rumor threads.

Named Entity Recognition The mainstream NER systems are designed to recognize flat entities and based on a sequence tagging framework. Collobert et al. (2011) introduced the linear-chain conditional random field (CRF) into neural networkbased sequence tagging models, which can explicitly encode the transition likelihoods between adjacent tags. Many researchers followed this work, and employed LSTM as the encoder. In addition, character-level representations are typically used for English tasks (Huang et al., 2015;Lample et al., 2016;Ma and Hovy, 2016;Chiu and Nichols, 2016), whereas lexicon information is helpful for Chinese NER (Zhang and Yang, 2018;Ma et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020a).

Nested NER allows a token to belong to multiple entities, which conflicts with the plain sequence tagging framework. Ju et al. (2018) proposed to use stacked LSTM-CRFs to predict from inner to outer entities. Straková et al. (2019) concatenated the BILOU tags for each token inside the nested entities, which allows the LSTM-CRF to work as for flat entities. Li et al. (2020b) reformulated nested NER as a machine reading comprehension task. Shen et al. (2021) proposed to recognize nested entities by the two-stage object detection method widely used in computer vision.

Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing.

In addition, pretrained language models, also known as contextualized embeddings, were also widely introduced to NER models, and significantly boosted the model performance (Peters et al., 2018;Devlin et al., 2019). They are used in our baseline by default.

Label Smoothing Szegedy et al. (2016) proposed the label smoothing as a regularization technique to improve the accuracy of the Inception networks on ImageNet. By explicitly assigning a small probability to non-ground-truth labels, label smoothing can prevent the models from becoming too confident about the predictions, and thus improve generalization. It turned out to be a useful alternative to the standard cross entropy loss, and has been widely adopted to fight against the over-confidence (Zoph et al., 2018;Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017;Vaswani et al., 2017), improve the model calibration (Müller et al., 2019), and denoise incorrect labels (Lukasik et al., 2020).

Our proposed boundary smoothing applies the smoothing technique to entity boundaries, rather than labels. This is driven by the observation that entity boundaries are more ambiguous and inconsistent to annotate in NER engineering. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first that focuses on the effect of smoothing regularization on NER models.
spans: Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing.

There have been several studies addressing technical solutions for aspect-based sentiment analysis. The emotional elements involved in ABSA mainly include aspect term, opinion term, aspect category, and sentiment polarity. In order to extract these emotional elements, the main research direction of ABSA is to extract aspect terms (Liu et al., 2015;Yin et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018;Ma et al., 2019) and categorize the sentiment of a given aspect (Wang et al., 2016;Chen et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2019;Zhang and Qian, 2020), and to jointly predict multiple elements simultaneously at the same time (Li et al., 2019a;Wan et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020).

The early ABSA problems were mostly expressed as sequence labeling or multi-classification problems (Li et al., 2019a), which were predicted by designing task-specific classification networks and using the class index as labels for training (Huang and Carley, 2019;Wan et al., 2020). However, this approach requires the design of different classification models and ignores the label semantics. Recent works achieve good performance by converting the ABSA problems as a text generation problem (Yan et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021a,b). A line of work frames ABSA as prompt-based generation with pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models, reporting gains from leveraging label semantics in natural language (Yan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Studies on domain adaptation focus on cross-domain robustness for aspect extraction and polarity transfer (He et al., 2018; Rietzler et al., 2020). Research on graph neural networks integrates syntactic dependencies to enhance aspect–opinion interactions (Wang et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2020). Benchmarks curate fine-grained annotations across domains to standardize evaluation (Pontiki et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2020). Crowdsourcing protocols explore agreement calibration for opinion targets (Akhtar et al., 2020).

In addition, the generative framework has been proven effective for many other natural language processing problems including dialogue state tracking (Feng et al., 2020), entity linking (De Cao et al., 2020), event extraction , information extraction (Sui et al., 2020), named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021;Tan et al., 2021;Raffel et al., 2019;Athiwaratkun et al., 2020). In particular, (Paolini et al., 2021) solved various NLP tasks in a unified generative framework.
spans: A line of work frames ABSA as prompt-based generation with pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models, reporting gains from leveraging label semantics in natural language (Yan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Studies on domain adaptation focus on cross-domain robustness for aspect extraction and polarity transfer (He et al., 2018; Rietzler et al., 2020). Research on graph neural networks integrates syntactic dependencies to enhance aspect–opinion interactions (Wang et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2020). Benchmarks curate fine-grained annotations across domains to standardize evaluation (Pontiki et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2020). Crowdsourcing protocols explore agreement calibration for opinion targets (Akhtar et al., 2020).

Robustness of MRC Models. The robustness of MRC models are usually evaluated against testtime perturbations and out-of-domain data. Research on test-time perturbation proposes perturbation methods at different levels as attacks (Si et al., 2021), such as word replacement with neighbors in the vector space (Rychalska et al., 2018;, question paraphrasing (Gan and Ng, 2019;Ribeiro et al., 2018), sentence distractor injection (Jia and Liang, 2017;Zhou et al., 2020). Another line of research (Fisch et al., 2019;Sen and Saffari, 2020) tests a model on data with out-of-domain passage or question distributions, usually from different datasets. Our work mainly falls into the category of test-time perturbation. We distinguish from previous work by focusing on the effect of entity replacement, with the motivation that entities can have flexible and diverse names in the real life.

Model Robustness to Entity Substitution. It is non-trivial for NLP models to be able to properly handle the large space of named entities. Previous works use entity substitution to audit or improve model robustness on different tasks like NER (Agarwal et al., 2020;Lin et al., 2021), Natural Language Inference (Mitra et al., 2020). Prior work on reading comprehension has explored adversarial triggers in passages (Jia and Liang, 2017) and stability to paraphrased questions (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Gan and Ng, 2019). Dense retrievers have also been evaluated under domain shifts to quantify retrieval degradation (Fisch et al., 2019), and calibration analyses report overconfidence under shallow lexical cues (Jiang et al., 2021). Studies on spurious correlations indicate that shortcut features can dominate evidence aggregation in extractive readers (Si et al., 2021; Gardner et al., 2021). Entity-centric datasets for coreference track whether models maintain identity across context windows (Wu et al., 2020). Sequence tagging approaches in NER with contextual encoders have been benchmarked for robustness to label noise (Peters et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019). In MRC specifically, entity substitution has been used to probe whether models truly ground answers in entity-linked evidence rather than surface cues. Augmentation strategies that replace named entities with type-consistent alternatives have been shown to reduce overfitting to frequent names while preserving answerability. Our approach builds on these insights by constructing minimally edited passages that preserve semantics but perturb entity surface forms. We further compare sensitivity across model families to isolate components that contribute to entity brittleness. Finally, we release a standardized evaluation protocol to facilitate reproducible measurement of substitution robustness across datasets and architectures.
spans: Prior work on reading comprehension has explored adversarial triggers in passages (Jia and Liang, 2017) and stability to paraphrased questions (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Gan and Ng, 2019). Dense retrievers have also been evaluated under domain shifts to quantify retrieval degradation (Fisch et al., 2019), and calibration analyses report overconfidence under shallow lexical cues (Jiang et al., 2021). Studies on spurious correlations indicate that shortcut features can dominate evidence aggregation in extractive readers (Si et al., 2021; Gardner et al., 2021). Entity-centric datasets for coreference track whether models maintain identity across context windows (Wu et al., 2020). Sequence tagging approaches in NER with contextual encoders have been benchmarked for robustness to label noise (Peters et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019).

Textual EL There is vast prior research on textual entity linking. Multiple datasets have been proposed over the years including the manuallyannotated high-quality datasets like AIDA (Hoffart et al., 2011), automatically-annotated large-scale datasets like CWEB (Guo and Barbosa, 2018) and zero-shot datasets like Zeshel (Logeswaran et al., 2019). To evaluate the EL models' performance, it is usual to train on the AIDA-train dataset, and test on the datasets of AIDA-test, MSNBC (Cucerzan, 2007), AQUAINT (Milne and Witten, 2008), etc. However, as mentioned in (Cao et al., 2021), many methods have achieved high and similar results within recent three years. One possible explanation is that it may simply be near the ceiling of what can be achieved for these datasets, and it is difficult to conduct further research based on them.

Multimodal EL In recent years, the growing trend towards multimodality requires to extend the research of EL from monomodality to multimodality. Moon et al. (2018)   Peng (2021) propose three MEL datasets 3 , which are built from Weibo, Wikipedia, and Richpedia information and use CNDBpedia, Wikidata and Richpedia as the corresponding KBs. Fang et al. (2022) introduced a dual-encoder retrieval model for cross-modal alignment. Chen and Li (2020) curated a dataset for visual commonsense reasoning with multimodal annotations. Zhu et al. (2019) explored transformer pretraining on large-scale image-text pairs. Wang et al. (2021) reported improvements in multimodal QA by leveraging knowledge graphs. However, using Wikipedia as the target dataset may lead to the data leakage problem as many language models are pretrained on it.

Our MEL dataset is also related to other named entity-related multimodal datasets, including entityaware image caption datasets (Biten et al., 2019;Tran et al., 2020;, multimodal NER datasets Lu et al., 2018), etc. However, the entities in these datasets are not linked to a unified KB. So our research of MEL can enhance the understanding of named entities, thereby enhancing the research in these areas.

(2018)   Peng (2021) propose three MEL datasets 3 , which are built from Weibo, Wikipedia, and Richpedia information and use CNDBpedia, Wikidata and Richpedia as the corresponding KBs. Fang et al. (2022) introduced a dual-encoder retrieval model for cross-modal alignment. Chen and Li (2020) curated a dataset for visual commonsense reasoning with multimodal annotations. Zhu et al. (2019) explored transformer pretraining on large-scale image-text pairs. Wang et al. (2021) reported improvements in multimodal QA by leveraging knowledge graphs.

document: Open-Domain Response Generation Recent work of open-domain response generation gener-ally follows the work of Ritter et al. (2011) where the task is treated as a machine translation task, and many of them use a Seq2Seq structure (Sutskever et al., 2014) following previous work (Vinyals and Le, 2015;Shang et al., 2015;Sordoni et al., 2015). In recent years, substantial improvements have been made (Serban et al., 2017;Li et al., 2016;Wolf et al., 2019), and embeddings are used to control response generation on extra information such as persona (Li et al., 2016), profiles (Yang et al., 2017), coherence (Xu et al., 2018, emotions (Huang et al., 2018), and dialogue attributes like response-relatedness (See et al., 2019). However, there is a lack of work that uses embeddings to control response generation over multiple corpora. Smith et al. (2020) survey retrieval-augmented generation for dialogue, and Weston et al. (2018) propose memory-augmented architectures for multi-turn contexts. Zhang et al. (2018) introduce controllable neural response generation with attribute-conditioned decoding. Dinan et al. (2019) construct Wizard-of-Wikipedia to ground conversations in knowledge. Our work follows the common models of opendomain conversational systems, while we study the problem of multiple corpora of different domains. To address this gap, we frame the setting as multi-corpus conditional generation, where corpus-specific signals are encoded as control variables. Prior efforts on domain adaptation in neural dialogue focus on style and topic transfer rather than corpus-driven control (Niu and Bansal, 2018;Zhang et al., 2020). We instead target a unified embedding interface that modulates decoding behavior across datasets with divergent annotation schemes. This perspective draws on advances in conditional text generation with disentangled representations (Hu et al., 2017;Shen et al., 2017). We contribute a control-space learning objective that balances corpus fidelity and response quality. Our approach complements persona-conditioned modeling by enabling corpus-aware conditioning that generalizes to unseen mixtures. We evaluate on multi-domain conversational benchmarks and report consistent gains in relevance and diversity. Ablations indicate that corpus-level control embeddings synergize with retrieval augmentation without sacrificing fluency. We release code and data to facilitate reproducible research and broader adoption.
spans: Smith et al. (2020) survey retrieval-augmented generation for dialogue, and Weston et al. (2018) propose memory-augmented architectures for multi-turn contexts. Zhang et al. (2018) introduce controllable neural response generation with attribute-conditioned decoding. Dinan et al. (2019) construct Wizard-of-Wikipedia to ground conversations in knowledge.

document: Given a sentence s = w 1 w 2 ...w n where w i is the ith token in the sentence, an OpenIE system generates triples of the form (subject; predicate; object), where subject, predicate and object are the constituents of a triple. Prior efforts on Open Information Extraction (OpenIE) emphasize robust tuple discovery from arbitrary sentences without domain constraints (Banko et al., 2007; Fader et al., 2011). Neural OpenIE variants leverage context-sensitive encoders to improve boundary detection and confidence estimation (Stanovsky et al., 2018; Kolluru et al., 2020). Pattern-driven methods refine canonicalization of surface predicates (Galárraga et al., 2014). These strands of research collectively motivate fine-grained modeling of argument spans and relation triggers in heterogeneous text. Several surveys synthesize advances in OpenIE evaluation and calibration metrics, underscoring the need for standardized benchmarks that test precision under lexical and syntactic drift (Etzioni et al., 2011; Stanovsky and Dagan, 2016). To address this, we explore training objectives that reconcile span-level precision with tuple-level completeness while maintaining generalization across domains. Work on argument structure identification addresses clause-level roles and span segmentation (He et al., 2018; Ouchi et al., 2018; Strubell et al., 2018). Dependency-based re-ranking can mitigate over-extraction by enforcing structural well-formedness (Christopoulou et al., 2019). Distant supervision aligns textual predicates with KB relations to scale supervision signals (Mintz et al., 2009; Zeng et al., 2015). Event extraction frameworks model triggers, arguments, and coreference chains (Ji and Grishman, 2008; Nguyen et al., 2016). Semantic parsing studies map utterances to executable forms useful for question answering (Berant et al., 2013; Pasupat and Liang, 2015). Finally, our approach integrates neural span scoring with predicate normalization to improve tuple consistency without sacrificing coverage on open-domain corpora.

Neural OpenIE variants leverage context-sensitive encoders to improve boundary detection and confidence estimation (Stanovsky et al., 2018; Kolluru et al., 2020). Pattern-driven methods refine canonicalization of surface predicates (Galárraga et al., 2014).

Work on argument structure identification addresses clause-level roles and span segmentation (He et al., 2018; Ouchi et al., 2018; Strubell et al., 2018). Semantic parsing studies map utterances to executable forms useful for question answering (Berant et al., 2013; Pasupat and Liang, 2015).

Given a set of entities E and a set of relations R, a knowledge graph K ⊆ E × R × E is a collection of subject-predicate-object (s, p, o) triples. Link prediction is the task of predicting missing triples in K by answering queries of the form of (s, p, ?) and (?, p, o). This is typically accomplished using knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models.

Conventional KGEs assign an embedding vector for each entity and relation in the KG. They model the plausibility of (s, p, o) triples via model specific scoring functions f (e s , e p , e o ) using the subject (e s ), predicate (e p ) and object (e o ) specific embeddings. Once trained, these embeddings are used for downstream tasks such as question answering.

Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) is the task of answering a natural language question using a KG as source of knowledge. The questions can be either simple factual questions that require a single fact retrieval (e.g. What language is spoken in India?), or they can be complex questions that require reasoning over multiple facts in the KG (e.g. What is the predominant religion where the leader is Ovadia Yosef?). KGEs can be utilized to perform KGQA when the background KGs are incomplete.

In the next few sections we will go into more detail about existing work on KGEs and KGQA. Recent embedding families include translational models, bilinear and tensor factorization models, and neural scoring functions, with early work popularized by TransE and TransH, followed by more expressive variants such as DistMult, ComplEx, and RESCAL, and more recent architectures like RotatE and box-based embeddings that capture asymmetric and hierarchical relations, and training strategies span margin-based ranking, sampled softmax, and adversarial negative sampling while evaluation has moved from raw hits@k to filtered MRR with careful attention to label leakage, and a concurrent line of research in textual entailment explored premise–hypothesis alignment with pre-trained transformers, datasets like SNLI and MNLI focused on sentence-level inference with annotations for contradiction and entailment, model calibration under distribution shift was measured through expected calibration error, and question paraphrase corpora like QQP have been used to probe semantic similarity representations. Across benchmarks such as FB15k-237, WN18RR, and YAGO3-10, stronger negative sampling and reciprocal relations often yield state-of-the-art link prediction. Recent studies also incorporate textual signals from encyclopedic sources to enrich entity representations and mitigate sparsity.

For KGQA, early pipeline systems map natural language questions to formal queries via semantic parsing over the KG schema. Neural approaches replace hand-crafted grammars with sequence-to-sequence parsers that generate logical forms or SPARQL queries. Multi-hop reasoning models leverage graph neural networks to aggregate evidence along candidate paths.

To better connect unstructured text with structured triples, retrieval-augmented methods condition the reasoning module on passages aligned with candidate entities. Dense retrievers trained on weak supervision improve recall of supporting facts for complex questions. Pre-trained language models fine-tuned with KG grounding signals demonstrate gains under compositional generalization.

Despite progress, challenges persist in handling ambiguity, incompleteness, and distribution shift in real-world KGs. Evaluation protocols must guard against train–test leakage from inverse relations and near-duplicates. Robustness analyses highlight the need for adversarial negatives that target spurious shortcuts.

Our work situates within this landscape by examining representations that unify link prediction signals with question-conditioned reasoning. We propose a training objective that balances structural plausibility with answer consistency under weak supervision. Empirically, we study generalization across question types, relation arity, and entity frequency.

We also investigate the data-efficiency of the approach under limited annotations. Few-shot adaptation is evaluated by sub-sampling question–answer pairs while maintaining KG topology. Finally, we report ablations that isolate contributions of structure-aware negatives and text-conditioned scoring.

We release code, pretrained checkpoints, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility. All experiments follow standardized splits and report both ranking and exact-match metrics. Detailed error analyses and calibration diagnostics are provided in the appendix.
spans: Recent embedding families include translational models, bilinear and tensor factorization models, and neural scoring functions, with early work popularized by TransE and TransH, followed by more expressive variants such as DistMult, ComplEx, and RESCAL, and more recent architectures like RotatE and box-based embeddings that capture asymmetric and hierarchical relations, and training strategies span margin-based ranking, sampled softmax, and adversarial negative sampling while evaluation has moved from raw hits@k to filtered MRR with careful attention to label leakage, and a concurrent line of research in textual entailment explored premise–hypothesis alignment with pre-trained transformers, datasets like SNLI and MNLI focused on sentence-level inference with annotations for contradiction and entailment, model calibration under distribution shift was measured through expected calibration error, and question paraphrase corpora like QQP have been used to probe semantic similarity representations.

Quantization of Pre-trained Language Models. Quantization compresses a model by representing the 32-bit floating-point parameter with a low-bit representation, and has been widely used in various domains as it does not require designing a new model architecture. There have been many attempts to quantize task-specific BERT models (Zafrir et al., 2019;Shen et al., 2020;Zadeh et al., 2020) with only negligible performance drop on natural language understanding tasks. Recent works Bai et al., 2021) even push the weight bit-width down to as low as 1-bit. Despite the success of these approaches for BERT models, at-tempts to quantize generative PLMs are scarce, and the underlying difficulty remains unclear.

Contrastive Learning. Contrastive learning aims at pushing the representations of similar samples together while pulling those of dissimilar ones apart. and is widely used for large-scale self-supervised learning in various domains Sun et al., 2020a;Baevski et al., 2020;Anonymous, 2022), and multi-modal learning (Radford et al., 2021;Jia et al., 2021). Sim-CLR  directly uses other in-batch samples as negatives, and sufficient large batch size is required to work well. MoCo  maintains a large number of negative samples in a queue and uses a moving average key encoder to improve consistency. Contrastive learning without negative samples is also proposed in BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen and He, 2021). Contrastive representation distillation (Tian et al., 2019) distills the knowledge from the teacher network to the student network by maximizing the mutual information between them.

The closest work with our token-level contrastive distillation is Wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), which use in-utterance representations at different positions as negatives in speech learning. Besides the difference in the modality and tasks, our method also differs from theirs in (1) Model: We quantize the model parameters and activations while they do not; (2) Representation: For each sample, we use the output of the full-precision and the quantized networks as its two views, while they use the quantized and the contextualized representation.

(3) Loss: We calculate loss over all tokens in an auto-regressive manner, while they only calculate over the masked tokens non-autoregressively.
spans: Recent studies on robust optimization introduce calibration-aware training for language models (Zheng et al., 2022) and methods for mitigating exposure bias during decoding (Welleck et al., 2020). Large-scale pretraining pipelines emphasize data curation strategies for stability (Gao et al., 2020), and adapter-based fine-tuning reduces memory overhead while preserving transfer (Houlsby et al., 2019). Extensive evaluations on summarization and question answering highlight sensitivity to tokenization granularity (Bostrom and Durrett, 2020).

document: Crafting adversarial examples and using them in training was previously shown to be an effective technique in improving NLP and ML models (Nie et al., 2020;Dinan et al., 2019;Kiela et al., 2021). Not only that, but adversarial attacks can reveal important vulnerabilities in our systems (Zhang et al., 2020a). Although previous work has studied adversarial examples in NLP (Li et al., 2017;Zang et al., 2020) most of them focused on accuracy as a metric of interest. Among the ones that studied toxicity and other ethical considerations (Wallace et al., 2019;Sheng et al., 2020) they did not put the focus on either conversational agents or they did not consider attacks being imperceptible. Cheng et al. (2019); Niu and Bansal (2018) studied adversarial attacks on conversational agents; however, their focus was on task oriented dialogue systems and also did not consider toxicity but accuracy as a metric. Xu et al. (2020) also considered conversational domains; however, they relied on human adversaries which can be costly and non-scalable. Recent studies on safety alignment emphasize content moderation and detoxification benchmarks (Gehman et al., 2020;Welbl et al., 2021), and large-scale pretraining has been linked to emergent robustness under distribution shift (Hendrycks et al., 2020). Prompt-based steering and red-teaming with LLMs have also been explored (Perez et al., 2022;Ganguli et al., 2022), and calibration techniques target uncertainty estimates (Guo et al., 2017). To address these gaps, we propose a framework for generating imperceptible adversarial perturbations that target toxicity in open-domain dialogue while preserving semantics and fluency, and we evaluate robustness–safety trade-offs under carefully controlled attack budgets and human preference judgments.

Prompt-based steering and red-teaming with LLMs have also been explored (Perez et al., 2022;Ganguli et al., 2022), and calibration techniques target uncertainty estimates (Guo et al., 2017).

Traditional sentiment analysis tasks are sentencelevel (Yang and Cardie, 2014;Severyn and Moschitti, 2015) or document-level (Dou, 2017;Lyu et al., 2020) oriented. In contrast, Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) is an aspect or entity oriented fine-grained sentiment analysis task. The most three basic subtasks are Aspect Term Extraction (ATE) (Hu and Liu, 2004;Yin et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018b;Xu et al., 2018;Ma et al., 2019;Chen and Qian, 2020;, Aspect Sentiment Classification (ASC) (Wang et al., 2016b;Tang et al., 2016;Ma et al., 2017;Fan et al., 2018;Li et al., 2018a;Li et al., 2021) and Opinion Term Extraction (OTE) Cardie, 2012, 2013;Fan et al., 2019;Wu et al., 2020b). The studies solve these tasks separately and ignore the dependency between these subtasks. Therefore, some efforts devoted to couple the two subtasks and proposed effective models to jointly extract aspect-based pairs. This kind of work mainly has two tasks: Aspect and Opinion Term Co-Extraction (AOTE) (Wang et al., 2016aDai and Song,  2019; Wang and Pan, 2019;Wu et al., 2020a) and Aspect-Sentiment Pair Extraction (ASPE) (Ma et al., 2018;Li et al., 2019a,b;He et al., 2019).

Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) first proposed the ASTE task and developed a two-stage pipeline framework to couple together aspect extraction, aspect sentiment classification and opinion extraction. To further explore this task, (Mao et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021a) transformed ASTE to a machine reading comprehension problem and utilized the shared BERT encoder to obatin the triplets after multiple stages decoding. Another line of research focuses on designing a new tagging scheme that makes the model can extract the triplets in an endto-end fashion Wu et al., 2020a;Xu et al., 2021;Yan et al., 2021). For instance,  proposed a positionaware tagging scheme, which solves the limitations related to existing works by enriching the expressiveness of labels. Wu et al. (2020a) proposed a grid tagging scheme, similar to table filling (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014;Gupta et al., 2016), to solve this task in an end-to-end manner. Yan et al. (2021) converted ASTE task into a generative formulation. However, these approaches generally ignore the relations between words and linguistic features which effectively promote the triplet extraction. Sun et al. (2019) introduced a contrastive pretraining objective for sentence representations, and Lee and Park (2020) examined domain adaptation effects in cross-lingual transfer with multilingual encoders. Johnson et al. (2017) explored zero-shot translation using shared subword vocabularies across languages. Nguyen and Smith (2018) studied attention calibration for interpretability in neural classifiers.

Recent advances in syntactic and semantic augmentation have shown benefits for structured prediction. Dependency trees and constituency spans offer complementary granularity for capturing opinion holders, targets, and modifiers. Several ABSA methods integrate dependency-guided attention to better align aspects with opinion words under long-distance dependencies. Incorporating part-of-speech and morphological cues has also improved robustness to domain-specific expressions in reviews. Joint modeling further reduces error propagation compared to pipeline baselines by sharing intermediate signals. End-to-end formulations can benefit from global constraints that enforce consistent sentiment polarity across multiple mentions. Span-based architectures unify extraction and classification by scoring candidate tuples under shared encoders. Pretrained language models provide strong initialization but require inductive biases tailored to relational extraction. Multi-task setups that include auxiliary objectives such as dependency edge prediction can regularize the triplet decoder. Distant supervision from review star ratings offers weak labels that complement limited token-level annotations. Data augmentation with paraphrase generation helps mitigate sparsity for rare aspect-opinion configurations. Curriculum strategies that begin with high-confidence pairs and gradually introduce harder cases stabilize training. Error analysis reveals that negation scope, implicit aspects, and comparative statements remain persistent challenges. Addressing these phenomena calls for models that integrate discourse cues and pragmatic knowledge. Domain adaptation remains important because vocabulary drift can degrade extraction fidelity across product categories. Benchmark consolidation and consistent evaluation protocols are needed to compare future systems. We follow this line by designing a unified tagging-and-scoring framework that fuses syntax-aware representations. Our experiments cover restaurant, laptop, and service domains with varying sentiment ambiguity. The results demonstrate gains over strong baselines on standard ASTE datasets. Ablations confirm the contribution of dependency features and span-level contrastive regularization. We release code and pretrained checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations related to bias and domain-specific language. The study concludes with directions for scalable, adaptable triplet extraction in practical applications. Our work emphasizes transparent reporting of hyperparameters and validation protocols. We believe these practices will help the community build comparable and extensible benchmarks. Future work may incorporate causal structure to disentangle correlated sentiment signals. Such developments could broaden the applicability of ASTE to multi-turn dialogues and multimodal reviews.

Johnson et al. (2017) explored zero-shot translation using shared subword vocabularies across languages. Nguyen and Smith (2018) studied attention calibration for interpretability in neural classifiers.

We describe related works on multi-document summarization categorized on the model architecture.

Flat solutions. Flat concatenation is a simple yet powerful solution because the multi-document summary generation is treated as a single-document summarization task, thus leveraging state-of-the-art pre-trained summarization models. Consequently, processing all documents as a flat input requires models capable of handling long sequences. As previously done by DeYoung et al. (2021), Xiao et al. (2021) propose to leverage the LongFormer-Encoder-Decoder model pre-trained with a novel multi-document summarization specific task. They proved that a long-range transformer that encodes all documents is a straightforward yet effective solution, and they achieved new state-of-the-art results in several multi-document summarization datasets. Such progress has coincided with explorations of pointer-generator mechanisms for factual consistency (See et al., 2017), redundancy-aware decoding strategies (Paulus et al., 2018), extract-then-abstract pipelines that segment inputs before fusion (Lebanoff et al., 2019), and memory-augmented seq2seq frameworks reported to stabilize long-context training (Fan et al., 2019). However, such models may struggle to handle a massive cluster of topic-related documents as they need to truncate them because of architectural limits. Further, processing all documents in a cluster could be noisy if some of them are not relevant or factual with respect to the final summary.

Hierarchical solutions. To better preserve crossdocument relations and obtain semantic-rich representations, hierarchical concatenation solutions leverage graph-based techniques to work from word and sentence-level (Wan and Yang, 2006;Liao et al., 2018;Nayeem et al., 2018;Antognini and Faltings, 2019; to documentlevel (Amplayo and Lapata, 2021). Other hierarchical approaches include multi-head pooling and inter-paragraph attention architectures (Liu and Lapata, 2019a), attention models with Maximal Marginal Relevance (Fabbri et al., 2019), and attention across different granularity representations (Jin et al., 2020). Prior work on cross-document temporal ordering (Wang and Cardie, 2013), entity-centric graph induction for news tracking (Christensen et al., 2013), and neural content planning with structured sketch representations (Moryossef et al., 2019) is also frequently cited. Such models are often datasetspecific because of the custom architecture, so they struggle to adapt to other datasets and effectively leverage pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers.

Our solution. In this work, we show how the summary-relevant information can be discriminated from a cluster of topic-related documents by a probabilistic neural method trained end-to-end. In particular, our solution fully leverages pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers and does not apply input truncation that causes performance drop and discards much important content, unacceptable for the medical domain.

Other hierarchical approaches include multi-head pooling and inter-paragraph attention architectures (Liu and Lapata, 2019a), attention models with Maximal Marginal Relevance (Fabbri et al., 2019), and attention across different granularity representations (Jin et al., 2020).
