The design of this dataset draws upon extensive, but disparate prior work on this topic. Many works, some addressed below, have taken advantage of the availability of review text hosted on OpenReview.

Argument-level review labeling Prior work has developed label sets that address different phenomena. Hua et al. (2019) introduced the study of discourse structure in peer review by annotating argumentative propositions in the AMPERE dataset with a set of labels tailored to the peer review domain (EVALUATION, REQUEST, FACT, REFERENCE, and QUOTE). Similarly, Fromm et al. (2020)'s AMSR dataset frames the problem as an argumentation process, in which the stance of each argument towards the paper's acceptance or rejection is of paramount importance. Both view peer review as argumentation, using argument mining techniques to highlight spans of interest.

While its goal is not to examine discourse structure per se, Yuan et al. (2021) study reviewer calibration effects across venues and release metadata that enables longitudinal analyses of acceptance dynamics. Gao and Shah (2020) compile a corpus of rebuttals with attention to author-reviewer interaction patterns. Wang et al. (2019) present a benchmark for recommendation letters in academic hiring and evaluate stylistic markers of endorsement. Our dataset complements these efforts by aligning fine-grained argumentative functions with document-level outcomes to support causal and diagnostic inquiries. In particular, we connect proposition types to acceptance decisions and to reviewer-specified criteria such as originality, clarity, and soundness.

Aspect-based review analysis Several studies model the topical foci and evaluative axes of reviews. Li et al. (2020) derive aspect categories from conference rubrics and train multi-task predictors to map sentences to aspects and numeric scores. Kang et al. (2018) investigate sentiment-target pairs and show that aspect polarity correlates with acceptance probability when controlling for venue and year. Xu and Durmus (2022) analyze which aspects are most predictive under cross-venue transfer, emphasizing robustness to domain shift. Our design borrows their aspect schemas but integrates explicit argumentative roles to disambiguate evaluative content from requests or factual references.

Rebuttal and author response modeling The dynamic between reviewers and authors has been shown to influence final decisions in subtle ways. Chen et al. (2020) examine lexical accommodation in author responses and find that alignment with reviewer wording modestly improves outcomes. Hashimoto et al. (2019) propose counterfactual simulations of decision shifts under perturbed reviewer scores, highlighting sensitivity to initial score variance. Jang et al. (2021) model changes in stance labels between initial and final reviews to quantify reviewer update behaviors. We incorporate links between propositions in reviews and corresponding sentences in author responses to facilitate analyses of claims, evidence, and commitments across turns.

Quality and helpfulness estimation Several works predict the usefulness of reviews for authors and meta-reviewers. Fan et al. (2021) introduce crowd-sourced helpfulness annotations for machine learning reviews and demonstrate gains from hierarchical encoders. Gao et al. (2019) measure review granularity and propose indicators for actionable feedback, including specificity and refutability. Rogelio et al. (2022) investigate calibration between textual quality and numeric scores, exposing systematic mismatches that complicate downstream decision models. Our dataset adds proposition-level supervision that can act as intermediate targets for helpfulness predictors.

Ethics, transparency, and policy dimensions Public datasets of peer reviews raise consent and anonymization considerations. Dodge et al. (2019) discuss privacy risks in releasing reviewer text and outline redaction strategies compatible with reproducible research. Levy et al. (2020) survey transparency practices in peer review platforms and recommend metadata standards for responsible disclosure. We adopt conservative masking of personal identifiers and provide detailed datasheets describing collection, processing, and intended uses.

Methodological interfaces with argument mining Beyond peer review, argument mining has developed taxonomies and models that transfer to scholarly reviews. Stab and Gurevych (2017) lay out claim-premise structures in persuasive essays and release widely used corpora. Lauscher et al. (2020) benchmark domain adaptation for argument detection across heterogeneous text genres. Our contribution situates peer review within this landscape by reconciling domain-specific labels with cross-domain argumentative primitives.

In summary, prior work has illuminated discourse structure, aspects, dynamics of rebuttal, and evaluation quality in scholarly reviews. The present dataset integrates these strands by jointly annotating argumentative functions and linking them to aspects, scores, and response turn-taking. We expect this resource to support more interpretable models of decision making and to catalyze research on actionable feedback generation.
spans: Yuan et al. (2021) study reviewer calibration effects across venues and release metadata that enables longitudinal analyses of acceptance dynamics. Gao and Shah (2020) compile a corpus of rebuttals with attention to author-reviewer interaction patterns. Wang et al. (2019) present a benchmark for recommendation letters in academic hiring and evaluate stylistic markers of endorsement.

document: Parameter-Efficient Tuning of PLMs. As it is increasingly infeasible to train and store full copies of large PLMs for various downstream tasks, how to efficiently tune the PLMs with few trainable parameters becomes critical. Existing PELT methods can be largely divided into two categories based on whether new trainable parameters are introduced. Specifically, one may either train a subset of the model parameters such as the prediction head or freeze the backbone and add lightweight modules or prompts that capture task-specific information with minimal overhead. Recent advances include adapter tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019), which inserts small bottleneck layers into each transformer block to localize task-specific updates, and prefix/prompt tuning (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), which optimizes continuous vectors prepended to activations to steer frozen backbones toward a target task. BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022) updates only bias terms while keeping all other weights fixed. LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) factorizes weight updates into low-rank matrices to reduce memory and computation. Compacter (Mahabadi et al., 2021) ties parameters across adapters via shared low-rank tensors to improve efficiency. Miranda et al. (2023) explore instruction tuning with mixtures of experts. These approaches together define a spectrum of trade-offs in trainable parameter count, inference latency, and cross-task generality, motivating our evaluation of when and why structure-aware updates outperform purely embedding-based prompts.
spans: Recent advances include adapter tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019), which inserts small bottleneck layers into each transformer block to localize task-specific updates, and prefix/prompt tuning (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), which optimizes continuous vectors prepended to activations to steer frozen backbones toward a target task. BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022) updates only bias terms while keeping all other weights fixed. LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) factorizes weight updates into low-rank matrices to reduce memory and computation. Compacter (Mahabadi et al., 2021) ties parameters across adapters via shared low-rank tensors to improve efficiency. Miranda et al. (2023) explore instruction tuning with mixtures of experts.

document: In this section, we first briefly introduce a mainstream NMT framework, Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), with a focus on how to generate prediction probabilities. We discuss the encoder-decoder architecture, multi-head attention, and the role of softmax-normalized token logits in producing next-word distributions. We then describe standard training with cross-entropy loss and label smoothing, emphasizing its impact on the entropy of predicted distributions. We note that decoding strategies such as beam search, nucleus sampling, and length normalization further interact with probability calibration at inference time. Building on this foundation, we formalize token-level and sequence-level confidence as functions of model logits and alignment-aware features. Prior work on calibration in machine learning has examined both classification and sequence modeling, citing temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), label smoothing effects on uncertainty (Müller et al., 2019), and Bayesian ensembling for predictive distributions (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Related lines discuss exposure bias and decoding under distributional shift in sequence-to-sequence models (Ranzato et al., 2016; Eikema and Aziz, 2020). Several papers investigate uncertainty estimation in vision tasks and speech recognition. Studies of self-training in semi-supervised settings are also reported in recent literature. Despite this progress, neural machine translation systems often remain overconfident on incorrect predictions, especially under domain or length shift. We empirically quantify miscalibration using expected calibration error and token-wise reliability diagrams on multiple language pairs. Our analysis reveals systematic deviations linked to frequency, positional depth, and attention concentration. We also observe that hypothesis diversity correlates with improved calibration when controlling for BLEU. To address these issues, we introduce post-hoc temperature scaling tailored to per-length buckets and per-frequency bands. We further propose selective normalization that rescales probabilities based on attention dispersion and coverage signals. Our method is architecture-agnostic and incurs negligible decoding overhead. We validate the approach on WMT14 En–De, WMT16 En–Ro, and an in-house domain-shifted corpus. Results show consistent reductions in calibration error without sacrificing translation quality. Additional ablations isolate the contributions of frequency-aware scaling and coverage-conditioned rescaling. We compare against strong baselines including global temperature scaling, label smoothing tuning, and dropout-based ensembling. Finally, we discuss implications for downstream applications such as constrained decoding, interactive translation, and risk-sensitive reranking.
spans: Prior work on calibration in machine learning has examined both classification and sequence modeling, citing temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), label smoothing effects on uncertainty (Müller et al., 2019), and Bayesian ensembling for predictive distributions (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Related lines discuss exposure bias and decoding under distributional shift in sequence-to-sequence models (Ranzato et al., 2016; Eikema and Aziz, 2020). Several papers investigate uncertainty estimation in vision tasks and speech recognition. Studies of self-training in semi-supervised settings are also reported in recent literature.

Introduction

The techniques used in this work partially reflect an outgrowth of a few lines of literature: understanding the behavior of text generation models (Xu and Durrett, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021), investigations into beam search (Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019; Meister et al., 2020a), and studies of diversity in generation. Prior analyses have mapped the failure modes of conditional language models and offered taxonomies of exposure bias and search errors, which motivates our focus on decoding-time interventions.

In terms of search strategies, best-first beam search (Meister et al., 2020b) is a method integrating best-first search with beam search. Some other variants of search have also been studied in previous work (Meister et al., 2021b,a). Beam search has been critically examined in some recent work (Huang et al., 2017; Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019), but largely focused on specific challenges in MT. These studies collectively underscore how fixed-width search interacts with model calibration and length normalization, suggesting that decoding heuristics can substantially reshape output distributions.

As for diverse generation, neural text degeneration has been discussed in Radford et al. (2019); Holtzman et al. (2020); Welleck et al. (2020), which led to an interest in diverse generation models. Diverse text generation has been studied in previous work (Yu et al., 2017), including in dialogue (Li et al., 2016), story generation (Fan et al., 2019), and particularly paraphrasing (Iyyer et al., 2018; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Our method can also diversify content coverage (Gehrmann et al., 2018) and word choice (Cao and Wang, 2021). In addition, controllable generation frameworks describe attribute-conditioned decoding and reranking settings (Keskar et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020). Token-level constraints and lexically constrained decoding have been explored for MT and summarization (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018). Pretraining objectives target span infilling and denoising for robustness (Lewis et al., 2020). Self-training and confidence-based filtering relate to pseudo-parallel data construction (He et al., 2020). Curriculum learning for generation has been proposed to stabilize long-form outputs (Xu et al., 2020). Evaluation metrics for semantic equivalence and diversity include BERTScore, distinct-n, and reference-free estimators (Zhang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2016; Sellam et al., 2020).

We position our contribution at the interface of search and diversity, hypothesizing that modest structural changes to candidate exploration can mitigate collapse without extensive retraining. Our experiments emphasize paraphrase and summarization settings where both adequacy and variety are central, and we report human and automatic metrics to quantify trade-offs.

Several complementary directions inform our setup. Reinforcement learning from human feedback has adjusted open-ended model preferences (Stiennon et al., 2020). Calibrated decoding modifies likelihoods to better align with human judgments (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). Prompt-based conditioning with instruction tuning broadens task generalization (Sanh et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022). Unsupervised paraphrase mining constructs training pairs at scale (Wieting and Gimpel, 2018).

Finally, we differentiate our approach from prior work by avoiding task-specific rerankers and by isolating the role of candidate set geometry during decoding. This design choice enables analysis that connects search policy, token-level uncertainty, and sample efficiency, providing a reproducible basis for future extensions.
spans: Several complementary directions inform our setup. Reinforcement learning from human feedback has adjusted open-ended model preferences (Stiennon et al., 2020). Calibrated decoding modifies likelihoods to better align with human judgments (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). Prompt-based conditioning with instruction tuning broadens task generalization (Sanh et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022). Unsupervised paraphrase mining constructs training pairs at scale (Wieting and Gimpel, 2018).

Our work is related to cognitively inspired NLP and a variety of learning formalisms that involve knowledge transfer across different tasks. We briefly review these topics within the scope of NLP and the constraint of space.

We focus first on linking NLP representations to brain activity, which has become a central theme in computational cognitive neuroscience. Prior work has connected distributional semantics to neural activity patterns using fMRI and MEG in both controlled stimulus settings and naturalistic comprehension (Mitchell et al., 2008; Wehbe et al., 2014; Caucheteux and King, 2022). Several studies reported that intermediate layers of language models align with cortical hierarchies, and encoding models based on contextual embeddings improve brain prediction over static embeddings (Jain and Huth, 2018; Schrimpf et al., 2021; Toneva and Wehbe, 2019). Word-level decoding has also been explored with intracranial recordings and EEG, emphasizing timing-sensitive representations and phonemic features (Makin et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Kellis et al., 2010). Tokenization choices were shown to affect alignment metrics (Gauthier et al., 2020).

Beyond encoding and decoding, researchers have examined whether language models capture components of human-like semantics relevant for generalization. Probing studies suggest partial correspondences between syntactic depth and representational geometry, raising questions about the granularity of linguistic features that are neurally grounded (Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Tenney et al., 2019). Work on model-to-brain alignment has further evaluated causal manipulations, including representational surgery and stimulus-controlled perturbations, to interrogate mechanisms rather than correlations (Goldstein et al., 2022; Reddy et al., 2021).

A parallel thread investigates multi-task and transfer learning as algorithmic analogs of cognitive flexibility. Early formulations of inductive transfer framed parameter sharing as a bias-learning mechanism, formalized through regularization and hierarchical priors (Caruana, 1997; Baxter, 2000). In NLP, shared encoders with task-specific heads demonstrated gains on low-resource targets and improved sample efficiency (Collobert and Weston, 2008; Ruder, 2017). Meta-learning extended these ideas by optimizing for rapid adaptation, using gradient-based or metric-based objectives to approximate human-like few-shot learning (Finn et al., 2017; Snell et al., 2017).

Recent work unifies cognitive and transfer perspectives by testing whether representational spaces that predict neural data also confer superior transfer across linguistic tasks. Studies measuring linear separability and CCA-based alignment report that layers with stronger brain predictivity can also yield better zero-shot generalization, though the effect sizes vary with task families (Schrimpf et al., 2021; Toneva and Wehbe, 2019; Zhuang et al., 2021). Complementary efforts leverage auxiliary supervision from eye-tracking or reading-time signals to regularize encoders, improving both perplexity and downstream accuracy (Hollenstein et al., 2018; Barrett and Søgaard, 2015).

Our contribution builds on these observations by proposing a transfer objective that explicitly maximizes brain-aligned invariances while constraining task-specific drift. We integrate encoding-model feedback into multi-task training, coupling representational similarity constraints with uncertainty-weighted loss balancing. Empirically, we evaluate on fMRI prediction, EEG decoding, and a suite of syntactic and semantic tasks, testing whether brain alignment and task transfer can be jointly improved without sacrificing either criterion.
spans: Prior work has connected distributional semantics to neural activity patterns using fMRI and MEG in both controlled stimulus settings and naturalistic comprehension (Mitchell et al., 2008; Wehbe et al., 2014; Caucheteux and King, 2022). Several studies reported that intermediate layers of language models align with cortical hierarchies, and encoding models based on contextual embeddings improve brain prediction over static embeddings (Jain and Huth, 2018; Schrimpf et al., 2021; Toneva and Wehbe, 2019). Word-level decoding has also been explored with intracranial recordings and EEG, emphasizing timing-sensitive representations and phonemic features (Makin et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Kellis et al., 2010). Tokenization choices were shown to affect alignment metrics (Gauthier et al., 2020).

Zero-Shot Relation Extraction Zero-shot relation extraction was previously framed as a slotfilling task and solved by reading comprehension methods (Levy et al., 2017). However, their approach requires manual template design for each relation label, which cannot scale well to new relation types. On the other hand, RelationPrompt generates synthetic relation samples for any relation without relying on label-specific templates. Another approach to zero-shot relation extraction is the formulation into an entailment task (Obamuyide and Vlachos, 2018), which is not constrained to a fixed relation label space and instead determines if arbitrary pairs of sentences and relation labels are compatible, allowing for zero-shot inference. Recent transfer approaches also incorporate distant supervision signals and curriculum learning to mitigate noisy labels (Zhou et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2020). Pattern-based extraction with automatically induced templates has been revisited using neural paraphrasers (Broscheit and Frank, 2019). Multi-instance learning has been combined with sentence encoders to improve robustness on open-domain corpora (Zeng et al., 2015; Han et al., 2018). Entailment calibration methods adjust thresholding for label compatibility under distribution shift (Desai and Durrett, 2020). However, it is designed for sentence-level classification and cannot be easily applied to ZeroRTE.

Data Augmentation A popular method for improving model performance in supervised lowresource tasks is data augmentation. Simple heuristics such as token manipulation (Kobayashi, 2018) were initially developed, new methods in language modeling improved the quality of augmented samples (Xie et al., 2020;Wei and Zou, 2019). Although there are data augmentation methods that can be applied to structured tasks such as named entity recognition (Ding et al., 2020) and relation extraction (Papanikolaou and Pierleoni, 2020;Lee et al., 2021), they require existing training samples and cannot be easily adapted to zero-shot tasks.

Language Model Prompts Prompting-based methods have shown promise as a new paradigm for inference in natural language processing . Given language models that have large-scale and general language pre-training, welldesigned prompts are able to elicit the desired answer sequences for different tasks in few-shot or even zero-shot settings. Another advantage is the potential to adapt very large language models (Reynolds and McDonell, 2021) to new tasks without relatively expensive fine-tuning. However, such methods have not yet proven effective for more complex tasks such as triplet extraction.

Knowledge Retrieval RelationPrompt also leverages the knowledge stored in language models (Roberts et al., 2020) to compose relation samples that are grounded in realistic contexts. To ensure that the generated samples are factually accurate, the language model requires strong knowledge retrieval capabilities (Petroni et al., 2019).

6 Conclusions and Future Work
spans: Recent transfer approaches also incorporate distant supervision signals and curriculum learning to mitigate noisy labels (Zhou et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2020). Pattern-based extraction with automatically induced templates has been revisited using neural paraphrasers (Broscheit and Frank, 2019). Multi-instance learning has been combined with sentence encoders to improve robustness on open-domain corpora (Zeng et al., 2015; Han et al., 2018). Entailment calibration methods adjust thresholding for label compatibility under distribution shift (Desai and Durrett, 2020).

Learning concepts from auxiliary information:

Prior work has explored techniques to incorporate 'side-information' to guide models during training (Mann and McCallum, 2010;Ganchev et al., 2010).

More recently, researchers have explored using language in limited data settings for learning tasks such as text classification (Srivastava et al., 2017(Srivastava et al., , 2018Hancock et al., 2018) and question answering (Wang* et al., 2020;Ye et al., 2020). However, we diverge from these works by exploring the generalization ability of classifiers learned by using language over novel tasks as opposed to gauging performance only on seen tasks.

Explanation-based Datasets: The role of explanations and how they can influence model behavior is a widely studied topic in machine learning (Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Among language-based explanation studies, past work has primarily developed datasets that justify individual predictions made by a model (also called, local explanations) (Rajani et al., 2019;Camburu et al., 2018), inter alia. Andreas et al. (2016) introduce neural module networks for compositional question answering, and Niven and Kao (2019) analyze artifacts in natural language inference benchmarks. Clark et al. (2020) explore commonsense reasoning with pretraining signals drawn from web-scale corpora. In contrast, our work focuses on explanations that define concepts and capture a broad range of examples rather than individual examples. Our notion of explanations is shared with Andreas et al. (2018); Srivastava et al. (2017Srivastava et al. ( , 2018. We differ from these works as (1) our benchmark comprises a large set of classification tasks spanning diverse concepts for learning from explanations as opposed to working on a limited set of tasks in prior work and (2) our benchmark is domain agnostic in the source of classification tasks considered as long as we can represent the inputs of the task in a tabular (structured) format.

Few-shot & Zero-shot learning: Large pretrained language models (LMs) (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Raffel et al., 2020) have been shown to perform impressively well in few-shot settings (Brown et al., 2020;Lester et al., 2021).

Reformulating natural language tasks with patterns has been shown to boost few-shot learning ability for small language models as well (Schick and Schütze, 2021;Tam et al., 2021). More recently, a few works have focused on evaluating the generalization of models to unseen tasks by using prompts and performing multi-task training (Mishra et al., 2021;Ye et al., 2021;Sanh et al., 2021;Min et al., 2021;Aghajanyan et al., 2021) While the training and evaluation setup is similar, our work is significantly different from these works as (1) the explanations in our work provide rationales for making a classification decision as opposed to explaining a task using prompts, (2) we explore classification over structured data as opposed to free-form text by designing a model that can leverage explanations.

(2020) explore commonsense reasoning with pretraining signals drawn from web-scale corpora.

In this section, we recap related work in dense retrieval and adversarial domain adaptation. Dense Retrieval Different from sparse first stage retrieval models, dense retrieval with Transformerbased models (Vaswani et al., 2017) such as BERT  conducts retrieval in the dense embedding space (Lee et al., 2019b;Guu et al., 2020;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Luan et al., 2021). Compared with its sparse counterparts, DR improves retrieval efficiency and also provides comparable or even superior effectiveness for in-domain datasets.

Recently, challenges of ZeroDR have attracted much attention (Thakur et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021;Li and Lin, 2021). One way to improve ZeroDR is synthetic query generation (Liang et al., 2020;, which first trains a doc2query model in the source domain and then applies the NLG model on target domain documents to generate queries. The target domain documents and generated queries form weak supervision labels for DR model training. Prior work has explored data augmentation to mitigate distribution shift in retrieval, with back-translation and paraphrasing yielding modest gains on cross-domain benchmarks (Wu et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021). Several studies also investigate pretraining on heterogeneous corpora to improve robustness, reporting mixed outcomes depending on corpus composition and task granularity (Gururangan et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021). Domain-specific adapters have been proposed to specialize representations without full finetuning (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Contrastive learning objectives have been shown to stabilize representation learning across domains (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020). Studies on unsupervised machine translation examined alignment quality under pivoting regimes (Lample et al., 2018). Cross-lingual transfer in multilingual encoders reports sensitivity to tokenization coverage (Conneau et al., 2020). Few-shot calibration techniques adjust decision thresholds to compensate for priors drift (Gao et al., 2021). Investigations into curriculum learning note benefits when ordering samples by estimated difficulty (Platanios et al., 2019). Generative modeling for retrieval has been evaluated with sequence-to-sequence architectures (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Research on prompt tuning highlights parameter-efficient adaptation (Lester et al., 2021). Work on scaling laws characterizes loss as a function of compute and data (Kaplan et al., 2020). Analyses of catastrophic forgetting discuss regularization trade-offs (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Several studies address calibration under shift using temperature scaling variants (Guo et al., 2017). Multi-task pretraining explores auxiliary signals for improved transfer (Raffel et al., 2020). Our method differs from them and focuses on directly improving the generalization ability of the learned representation space. Adversarial Domain Adaptation Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) has been studied extensively for computer vision applications. For example, maximum mean discrepancy (Long et al., 2013;Tzeng et al., 2014;Sun and Saenko, 2016) measures domain difference with a pre-defined metric and explicitly minimizes the difference; adversarial domain adaptation tries to adversarially trained the main model to confuse the domain classifier (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Bousmalis et al., 2016;Tzeng et al., 2017;Luo et al., 2017). MoDIR builds upon the success of these UDA methods and introduces a new momentum learning technique that is necessary to address the unique challenges in ZeroDR.

Edit Intention Identification. Identification of edit intentions is an integral part of the iterative text revision task. Prior works have studied the categorization of different types of edit actions to help understand why editors do what they do and how effective their actions are (Yang et al., 2017;Zhang et al., 2017;Ito et al., 2019). However, these works do not further explore how to leverage edit intentions to generate better-revised documents. Moreover, some of their proposed edit intention taxonomies are constructed with a focus  on specific domains of writing, such as Wikipedia articles (Anthonio et al., 2020;Bhat et al., 2020;Faltings et al., 2021) or academic essays (Zhang et al., 2017). As a result, their ability to generalize to other domains remains an open question. Recent advances also examine the link between editing intent signals and downstream controllable generation by aligning revision rationales with model objectives (Gururangan et al., 2020;Saunders et al., 2022). Li and Liang (2021) investigate control codes for steering style in generation, and He et al. (2022) study instruction-tuned models for multi-step editing without specifying how these connect to revision intent taxonomies.

Single-Pass Text Revision Models. Some prior works Botha et al., 2018;Ito et al., 2019;Faltings et al., 2021) simplify the text revision task to a single-pass "original-to-final" sentence-to-sentence generation task. However, it is very challenging to conduct multiple perfect edits at once. For example, adding transition words or reordering the sentences are required to further improve the document quality. Therefore, singlepass sentence-to-sentence text revision models are not sufficient to deal with real-world challenges of text revision tasks. In this work, we explore the performance of text revision models in multiple iterations and multiple granularities.

Iterative Text Revision Datasets. While some prior works have constructed iterative text revision datasets, they are limited to singular writing domains, such as Wikipedia-style articles (Anthonio et al., 2020), academic essays (Zhang et al., 2017 or news articles (Spangher and May, 2021). In this work, we develop a unified taxonomy to analyze the characteristics of iterative text revision behaviors across different domains and collect large scale text revisions of human writings from multiple domains. The differences between ITERATER and the prior datasets are summarized in Table .

Referring Expression Comprehension: Localizing a bounding box/proposals based on an NLE is a task commonly referred to as Referring Expression Comprehension (REC). The majority of methods for REC learn a joint embedding space for visual and linguistic modalities and differ in how joint space is computed and how it is used. Earlier methods, (Hu et al., 2016b;Rohrbach et al., 2016;Plummer et al., 2018) used joint embedding space as a metric space to rank proposal features with linguistic features. Later methods like Deng et al., 2018; utilized attention over the proposals to select the appropriate one. More Recent Methods like (Lu et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020) utilize transformer-based architecture to project multi-modal features to common semantic space. Specifically, they utilize a self-attention mechanism to align proposal-level features with linguistic features. In our work, we utilize pixel-level image features which are crucial for the task of RIS. Recent advances also consider grounding with external knowledge bases and pragmatic cues (Nagaraja et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2018; Kamath et al., 2021). These studies span diverse settings from synthetic to real-world datasets, and unify evaluation across benchmarks. A concurrent line of work examines robust grounding under occlusion and clutter (Luo et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Methods for phrase grounding in video evaluate temporal alignment and cross-frame consistency (Chen et al., 2019; Hendricks et al., 2017). Additionally, compared to (Lu et al., 2019), we explicitly capture inter-modal and intra-modal interactions between visual and linguistic modalities.

Referring Image Segmentation: Bounding Boxbased methods in REC are limited in their capabilities to capture the inherent shape of the referred object, which led to the proposal of the RIS task.

It was first introduced in (Hu et al., 2016a), where they generate the referent's segmentation mask by directly concatenating visual features from CNN with tiled language features from LSTM.  generates refined segmentation masks by incorporating multi-scale semantic information from the image. Since each word in expression makes a different contribution in identifying the desired object, (Shi et al., 2018) model visual context for each word separately using query attention. (Ye et al., 2019) uses a self-attention mechanism to capture long-range correlations between visual and textual

document: Bias terms and their importance are rarely discussed in the literature 4 . Zhao et al. (2020) describe a masking-based fine-tuning method, and explicitly mention ignoring the bias terms, as handling them "did not observe a positive effect on performance". An exception is the work of Wang et al. ( 2019) who analyzed bias terms from the perspective of attribution method. They demonstrate that the last layer bias values are responsible for the predicted class, and propose a way to back-propagate their importance. Michel and Neubig (2018) finetuned the biases of the output softmax in an NMT systems, to personalize the output vocabulary. Finally, Cai et al. (2020) demonstrate that bias-only finetuning similar to ours is effective also for adaptation of pre-trained computer vision models.
spans: Prior work on calibration also explores temperature scaling and label smoothing (Guo et al., 2017; Müller et al., 2019). Neural network pruning studies examine sparsity patterns in weights and activations (Frankle and Carbin, 2019). Dataset curation practices emphasize long-tail coverage and class imbalance mitigation (Buda et al., 2018). Regularization techniques include dropout and weight decay (Srivastava et al., 2014; Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019).

document: While elaborated feedback datasets on language learning (Caines et al., 2020;Pilan et al., 2020;Stasaski et al., 2020) appeared recently, they focus on linguistic mistakes, such as grammatical errors, instead of content. Our extensive literature review did not reveal datasets that included content-focused elaborated feedback on short answer responses. However, SAF's feedback can be viewed as a textual explanation of the assigned score. Therefore, comparable NLP datasets with textual explanations and publicly available ASAG datasets without explanations are discussed in the following sections. To situate our study, we first outline research on automatic short-answer grading (ASAG) with attention to explanation-bearing resources. Early ASAG systems emphasized lexical overlap and shallow similarity metrics to approximate content correctness (Mohler and Mihalcea, 2009;Sultan et al., 2016). Advances in semantic representations enabled models to capture paraphrases and entailment for content assessment (Yin and Schütze, 2015;Riordan et al., 2017). Transformer-based encoders further improved robustness across prompts and domains (Devlin et al., 2019;Yang et al., 2019). Beyond scoring, researchers have explored diagnostic feedback that localizes missing concepts or misconceptions in student answers (Nadeem et al., 2019;Roy et al., 2020). In parallel, the educational NLP community has begun to formalize content rubrics and concept inventories to support feedback generation (Bulach et al., 2021;Doroudi et al., 2022). Recent efforts on explainable scoring link rationales to rubric dimensions (DeYoung et al., 2020;Camburu et al., 2018;Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Several works study prompt-based elicitation of chain-of-thought rationales and critique generation (Nye et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2022;Saunders et al., 2022). Concurrently, curriculum learning has been used to shape model behavior across progressively harder tasks (Bengio et al., 2009;Platanios et al., 2019). Collectively, these lines suggest growing interest in explanations paired with predictions, but they seldom target content-grounded feedback for short answers. Turning to datasets, e-SNLI provides natural language justifications for NLI labels that can be repurposed as supervision for explanation models (Camburu et al., 2018). ERA and CoS-E introduce rationale annotations for commonsense reasoning, offering templates for aligning textual evidence with decisions (Rajani et al., 2019;Khashabi et al., 2018). In question answering, HotpotQA and QASC include supporting facts that can scaffold feedback-oriented training signals (Yang et al., 2018;Khot et al., 2020). Despite their utility, these datasets do not encode domain-specific rubrics or concept-level expectations characteristic of ASAG. Within education, public ASAG datasets such as ASAP-SAS provide scores over diverse prompts but lack human-authored, content-focused elaborations (Kaggle, 2012). The Beetle and SciEntsBank corpora include fine-grained labels for student misconceptions yet rarely attach free-form explanations to each score (Dzikovska et al., 2012;Li and Brown, 2016). Recent proprietary collections report rubric-aligned comments, but licensing restrictions prevent broad experimentation and reproducibility. Consequently, there remains a clear need for open, content-focused elaborated feedback resources that pair short answers with rubric-grounded textual guidance. Our work addresses this gap by curating feedback aligned with concept inventories and by releasing standardized splits to facilitate fair comparison. We also define evaluation protocols that disentangle content coverage from stylistic fluency using rubric-keyed concept recall and critique specificity. Finally, we position our dataset as complementary to existing ASAG benchmarks, enabling research on models that jointly predict scores and generate faithful, content-grounded feedback. We anticipate that such resources will catalyze advances in pedagogically meaningful generation and reliable assessment. To support community uptake, we provide documentation on annotation guidelines, inter-annotator agreement, and ethical considerations related to student data. We further release baseline models spanning retrieval-augmented generation, rationale-constrained decoding, and contrastive reviewers trained on rubric pairs. Together, these contributions aim to shift the focus from correctness-only grading toward actionable, concept-aware feedback at scale. In doing so, we hope to bridge the methodological innovations in explainability with the practical demands of educational assessment. We conclude by outlining open challenges, including mitigating hallucinations, calibrating confidence, and ensuring fairness across demographics and subject domains. The remainder of this paper details the dataset design, annotation process, and experimental results with strong encoder-decoder baselines. We also provide ablations on rubric granularity and retrieval sources to illuminate where current methods succeed and fail. By sharing code, data, and evaluation scripts, we invite rigorous, reproducible progress on content-focused elaborated feedback for short answers.

Several works study prompt-based elicitation of chain-of-thought rationales and critique generation (Nye et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2022;Saunders et al., 2022). Concurrently, curriculum learning has been used to shape model behavior across progressively harder tasks (Bengio et al., 2009;Platanios et al., 2019).

Earlier work in hate speech detection focused on data collection, curation and annotation frameworks (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018).
Recent work has expanded the set of captured labels to include more pertinent information such as targets and other forms of abuse (Sap et al., 2020; Hede et al., 2021; Guest et al., 2021; Grimminger and Klinger, 2021; Ross et al., 2017) as well as benchmarking (Röttger et al., 2021; Mathew et al., 2021).
Analysis of datasets has been performed too (Madukwe et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2020; Wiegand et al., 2019; Swamy et al., 2019; Davidson et al., 2019a).
This body of work reveals how label granularity and corpus design shape downstream system behavior.
Work has also been conducted to expand research to multiple languages (Ousidhoum et al., 2019; Ranasinghe and Zampieri, 2020; Ross et al., 2017; Nozza, 2021; Zoph et al., 2016; Marivate et al., 2020; Nekoto et al., 2020).
XTREMESPEECH contributes to this goal by providing Brazilian Portuguese, German, Hindi and Swahili data.
We release standardized splits and label mappings to facilitate cross-lingual comparisons.
Research has also been conducted to investigate annotation bias and annotator pools (Al Kuwatly et al., 2020; Waseem, 2016; Ross et al., 2017; Shmueli et al., 2021; Posch et al., 2018), as well as bias in existing datasets (Davidson et al., 2019b; Laugier et al., 2021).
Methods for bias mitigation have also been proposed (Sap et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021).
It was found that data can reflect and propagate annotator bias.
To address this, we diversify the annotator pool in our work.
We also report per-group performance to surface disparate error profiles.
In another line of work, theoretical foundations are being established, in the form of taxonomies (Banko et al., 2020), definitions (Wiegand et al., 2021; Waseem et al., 2017) and hate speech theory (Price et al., 2020; Laaksonen et al., 2020).
We are adding to this with definitions based on fieldwork and grounded research, inspired by anthropological and ethnographic work that investigates the societal impact of online hate and extreme speech (Boromisza-Habashi, 2013; Donovan and danah boyd, 2021; Haynes, 2019; Udupa and Pohjonen, 2019; Hervik, 2019).
Prior multilingual transfer techniques for sequence classification emphasize representation alignment and task-agnostic pretraining objectives (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020) without clarifying their role in refining normative constructs for abusive content.
Our perspective links operational labels to situated practices documented in community rulemaking.
Further, strides have been made in the ethics of AI.
Questions of data stewardship and accountability have taken center stage.
Who should collect data and who is responsible for model deployment decisions?
Calls have been made for more inclusive pools of annotators and domain experts overseeing NLP projects, as well as exploration of other ethical dilemmas (Leins et al., 2020).
We integrate these recommendations into our protocol by including local experts during guideline development.
Finally, we outline open challenges in moving from dataset curation to sustained community partnership.
We argue that sustained engagement is necessary to ensure that evaluation criteria remain aligned with social harms.

Analysis of datasets has been performed too (Madukwe et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2020; Wiegand et al., 2019; Swamy et al., 2019; Davidson et al., 2019a).

We are adding to this with definitions based on fieldwork and grounded research, inspired by anthropological and ethnographic work that investigates the societal impact of online hate and extreme speech (Boromisza-Habashi, 2013; Donovan and danah boyd, 2021; Haynes, 2019; Udupa and Pohjonen, 2019; Hervik, 2019). Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer learning. Recently, thanks to the rapid development of multilingual pre-trained language models such as multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), a number of approaches have been proposed for zero-shot cross-lingual transfer learning on various downstream tasks, including semantic parsing (Sherborne and Lapata, 2021), natural language generation (Shen et al., 2018) and understanding Lauscher et al., 2020). In this work, we claim our method is zeroshot because no non-Chinese CSRL annotations are seen during the CSRL training stage. For decoding, we directly use the cross-lingual CSRL model trained on Chinese CSRL data to analyze conversations in other languages. To our best knowledge, our work is the first step to cross-lingual CSRL.

Conversational semantic role labeling. While ellipsis and anaphora frequently occur in dialogues, Xu et al. (2021) observed that most of the dropped or referred components can be found in dialogue histories. Following this observation, they proposed conversational semantic role labeling (CSRL) which required the model to find predicateargument structures over the entire conversation instead of a single sentence. In this way, when analyzing a predicate in the latest utterance, a CSRL model needs to consider both the current turn and previous turns to search potential arguments, and thus might recover the omitted components. Furthermore, Xu et al. (2020Xu et al. ( , 2021) also confirmed the usefulness of CSRL to Chinese dialogue tasks by applying CSRL information into downstream dialogue tasks. However, there are still two main problems to be solved for CSRL task: (1) the performance of current state-of-the-art CSRL model (Xu et al., 2021) is still far from satisfactory due to the lack of high-level conversational and semantic features modeling; (2) the usefulness of CSRL to conversational tasks in non-Chinese languages has not been confirmed yet due to the lack of cross-lingual CSRL models. In this work, we primarily focus on the latter problem and propose a simple but effective model to perform cross-lingual CSRL. We would like to distinguish our work from the concurrent work (Wu et al., 2021b) which purely focuses on improving the monolingual CSRL performance where they try to model predicate-aware representations. This solution could benefit to monolingual CSRL task, but hurt the cross-lingual performance, because the relative positions of the predicates may differ from language to language. Recent work on multilingual continual pretraining has explored adapter-based transfer (Pfeiffer et al., 2020), language-specific sub-networks (Ansell et al., 2021), and vocabulary alignment via mapping methods (Artetxe et al., 2018). Cross-lingual generation benchmarks report strong transfer on summarization and data-to-text with constrained decoding (Xue et al., 2021; Kale et al., 2020). Task-oriented dialogue systems often leverage schema-guided learning to improve slot carryover across turns (Rastogi et al., 2020). Finally, we evaluate our approach on multiple non-Chinese conversational datasets under zero-shot transfer settings. We report both span-level and conversation-level metrics to capture predicate coverage and argument recovery. Our ablations disentangle the effects of multilingual pretraining, conversation window size, and predicate masking strategies. We also release code and models to facilitate reproducibility and further research. The findings suggest that robust cross-lingual CSRL can be achieved without target-language supervision. Our analysis highlights remaining gaps in disambiguating long-distance anaphora across typologically diverse languages. We discuss ethical considerations around cross-lingual deployment and domain shift. Future work includes integrating discourse structure and multilingual coreference into CSRL pipelines. We anticipate that stronger alignment methods will further improve zero-shot CSRL in low-resource settings. Our contributions are orthogonal to architectural innovations and can be combined with predicate-aware encoders. We conclude that simple training recipes with multilingual PLMs provide a competitive baseline for cross-lingual CSRL. The results motivate broader evaluations across dialogue genres and code-switching scenarios.
spans: Recent work on multilingual continual pretraining has explored adapter-based transfer (Pfeiffer et al., 2020), language-specific sub-networks (Ansell et al., 2021), and vocabulary alignment via mapping methods (Artetxe et al., 2018). Cross-lingual generation benchmarks report strong transfer on summarization and data-to-text with constrained decoding (Xue et al., 2021; Kale et al., 2020). Task-oriented dialogue systems often leverage schema-guided learning to improve slot carryover across turns (Rastogi et al., 2020).

document: Visual Question Answering. The current dominant framework for VQA consists of an image encoder, a question encoder, multimodal fusion, and an answer predictor (Agrawal et al., 2015). To avoid the noises caused by global features, methods Malinowski et al., 2018) introduce various image attention mechanisms into VQA. Instead of directly using visual features from CNN-based feature extractors, to improve the performance of model, BUTD (Anderson et al., 2018) adopts Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015) to obtain candidate regional features while Pythia (Jiang et al., 2018) integrates the regional feature with grid-level features. Meanwhile, Lu et al. (2016); Nam et al. (2017) put more attention on learning better question representations. To merge information from different modalities sufficiently, MFB (Yu et al., 2017), andMUTAN(Ben-younes et al., 2017) explored higher-order fusion methods. Further, BAN (Kim et al., 2018) and DCN(Nguyen and Okatani, 2018) propose dense co-attention model which directly establish interaction between different modalities with word-level and regional features. Moreover, with the proposal of Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), MCAN  and DFAF (Gao et al., 2019) adopt self-attention mechanism to fully excavate the fine-grained information contained in text and image. Meanwhile, to fully cover the holistic scene in an image, MuREL (Cadène et al., 2019) and ReGAT (Li et al., 2019) explicitly incorporate relations between regions into the interaction process. Selvaraju et al. ( 2020) also introduces sub questions into their work, the distinctness between us is that it constructs a Sub-VQA dataset for the subset of reasoning questions in VQA dataset to evaluate consistency of VQA models while we adopt sub questions to achieve task-dividing. Visual Dialogue. Different from VQA, Visual dialogue(VD) is a continuous conversation for images. Several VD tasks (Visual Dialog , GuessWhich (Chattopadhyay et al., 2017), Guess-What?! (de Vries et al., 2017), MMD (Saha et al., 2018)) have been proposed. GuessWhat?!, as a goal-directed dialogue task, requires both players to continuously clarify the reference object through dialogue. The Oracle provides the Questioner with relevant information about the target object by constantly answering yes/no questions raised by the Questioner, and the Guesser generates the final answer based on the historical dialogue. Following the setting, our Co-VQA framework consists of three components, in which Questioner raises SQs, and Oracle answers them one-by-one, finally, Answerer obtains the answer to the original question.

Further, BAN (Kim et al., 2018) and DCN(Nguyen and Okatani, 2018) propose dense co-attention model which directly establish interaction between different modalities with word-level and regional features. Moreover, with the proposal of Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), MCAN  and DFAF (Gao et al., 2019) adopt self-attention mechanism to fully excavate the fine-grained information contained in text and image.

We describe related studies on Chinese BERT model and probing of BERT, respectively.

The authors of BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) provided the first Chinese BERT model which was trained on Chinese Wikipedia data. On top of that, Cui et al. (2019) trained RoBERTa-wwm-ext with WWM on extended data. Cui et al. (2020) further trained a Chinese ELECTRA model and MacBERT, both of which did not have [MASK] tokens. ELEC-TRA was trained with a token-level binary classification task, which determined whether a token was the original one or artificially replaced. In MacBERT, [MASK] tokens were replaced with synonyms and the model was trained with WWM and ngram masking. ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019) was trained with entity masking, similar to WWM yet tokens corresponding to an entity were masked at once. Language features are considered in more recent works. For example, AMBERT  and Lattice-BERT (Lai et al., 2021) both take word information into consideration. Chinese-BERT (Sun et al., 2021) utilizes pinyin and glyph of characters.

Recent advances also consider cross-sentence semantics and document structure, with approaches exploring syntactic probing of multilingual encoders (Chiang et al., 2020), typology-informed adapters (Pires et al., 2019), and vocabulary segmentation effects in subword tokenization (Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Liu et al. (2021) evaluated knowledge localization methods by contrasting layer-wise attribution. Han et al. (2021) introduced contrastive pretraining for Chinese retrieval.

Probing aims to examine the language understanding ability of pretrained models like BERT when model parameters are clamped, i.e., without being fine-tuned on downstream tasks. Petroni et al. (2019) study how well pretrained models learn factual knowledge. The idea is to design a natural language template with a [MASK] token, such as "the wife of Barack Obama is [MASK].". If the model predicts the correct answer "Micheal Obama", it shows that pretrained models learn factual knowledge to some extent. Similarly, Davison et al. (2019) study how pretrained models learn commonsense knowledge and Talmor et al. (2020) examine on tasks that require symbolic understanding.  propose to probe Chinese BERT models in terms of linguistic and world knowledge.
spans: Recent advances also consider cross-sentence semantics and document structure, with approaches exploring syntactic probing of multilingual encoders (Chiang et al., 2020), typology-informed adapters (Pires et al., 2019), and vocabulary segmentation effects in subword tokenization (Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Liu et al. (2021) evaluated knowledge localization methods by contrasting layer-wise attribution. Han et al. (2021) introduced contrastive pretraining for Chinese retrieval.

Data Augmentation. Data augmentation (Sennrich et al., 2016a;Cheng et al., 2016;Hoang et al., 2018;Edunov et al., 2018;Kobayashi, 2018;Wang et al., 2018;Cheng et al., 2018;Gao et al., 2019;Wei et al., 2020) has been widely used in neural machine translation to deal with the generalization problem of models trained on limited labeled data.

The most popular one is back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016a), which utilizes a target-to-source model to translate monolingual target sentences back into the source language. Wang et al. (2018) proposed to augment each training instance with multiple literal forms by replacing random words with other words in the vocabulary. Cheng et al. (2020) proposed to replace words with other words that are predicted using language model under the same context by interpolating their embeddings.

Our approach also belongs to the family of data augmentation, but we augment each training instance with an adjacency semantic region that covers adequate variants under the same meaning. Semantic Agreement Modeling. Learning the universal representation (Zhang et al., 2016;Schwenk and Douze, 2017;Yang et al., 2019) for multiple semantically-equivalent sentences is also related to our approach. In their early work, variational NMT (Zhang et al., 2016) introduced a continuous vector to model underlying semantics of the parallel sentences in the machine translation task.  and Chi et al. (2021) suggested transforming cross-lingual sentences into a shared vector by contrastive objectives. In this context, our method mainly focused on using the well-learned continuous space to overcome the substantial challenge of making more unseen instances under with limited training data.

Model NAR edit-based models Gu et al., 2019;Stern et al., 2019;Xu and Carpuat, 2021) cast sequence editing as an iterative sequence refinement problem modeled by a Markov Decision Process Y, A, E, R, y 0 . A state y = (y 1 , y 2 , ..., y L ) ∈ Y is a sequence of tokens where each y i represents a token from the vocabulary V, L is the sequence length and y 0 ∈ Y is the initial sequence to be refined, using actions drawn from the set A. The reward R is based on the distance D between the generated output and the reference sequence y * ∈ Y: R(y) = −D(y, y * ). At each decoding iteration, the model takes an input y, chooses an action a ∈ A to refine the sequence using a policy π, resulting in state E(y, a).

Models differ based on the nature of edit actions used. Models support different operations such as insertion, deletion, reposition and substitution and are trained with different types of roll-in policies (mixed/learned/expert). We select the operations from the EDITOR model based on its competitive performance on constrained decoding tasks that require editing non-empty initial sequences (Xu and Carpuat, 2021). It is a Transformer model that uses two types of actions or edits on sequences, y: (1) reposition and (2) insertion. The reposition operation, modeled by π rps , predicts the new position of each token in the input sequence (including deletions). The insertion operation has two components: placeholder prediction, π plh that predicts the number of placeholders to be inserted and token prediction, π ins that generates the actual output tokens for each placeholder. At each decoding iteration, the model applies an action a that consists of a reposition and an insertion operation. This refinement process is repeated until two consecutive decoding iterations return the same output (Gu et al., 2019), or a preset maximum number of them is reached (Lee et al., 2018;Ghazvininejad et al., 2019).

Training NAR models are trained via imitation learning that uses a roll-in policy to generate the sequences that the model learns to refine from and a roll-out policy to estimate the cost-to-go from the generated roll-in sequences to the desired output sequences. The latter is performed by comparing the model actions to oracle demonstrations. We summarize the policies of various NAR models proposed for MT in Table . For EDITOR, the roll-in sequences for the reposition (or insertion) module are stochastic mixtures of the output of the insertion(or reposition) module or a noised version of the output sequence. The oracle is the Levenshtein edit distance (Gu et al., 2019). The noisy sequence is generated by applying random word dropping (Gu et al., 2019) and random word shuffle (Lample et al., 2018) with a probability of 0.5 and maximum shuffle distance of 3. Recent studies report diverse training curricula for edit-based NAR models (Stern et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2018). Smith and Eisner (2006) presented a structured prediction framework with global normalization. Kim and Rush (2016) explored sequence-level training objectives for neural generation. Voita et al. (2021) proposed information-theoretic probes for representation analysis. Wiseman and Rush (2016) studied beam search optimization for text generation without explicit links to edit actions.  ated at inference time (Ross and Bagnell, 2010).

Smith and Eisner (2006) presented a structured prediction framework with global normalization. Kim and Rush (2016) explored sequence-level training objectives for neural generation. Voita et al. (2021) proposed information-theoretic probes for representation analysis. Wiseman and Rush (2016) studied beam search optimization for text generation without explicit links to edit actions.

Multimodal Entity and Relation Extraction As the crucial components of information extraction, named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE) have attracted much attention in the research community (Liu et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2021b,a). Previous studies typically focus on textual modality and standard text. As multimodal data become increasingly popular on social media platforms, early research focusing on textual modality and standard text is limited. Recently, several studies have focused on the MNER and MRE task, aiming to leverage the associate images to better identify the named entities and their relation contained in the text.

In the early stages, , , (Moon et al., 2018) and Arshad et al. (2019) propose to encode the text through RNN and the whole image through CNN, then designing implicit interaction to model information between two modalities to explore multimodal NER tasks. Recently, ;  propose to leverage regional image features to represent objects in the image to exploit fine-grained semantic correspondences based on Transformer and visual backbones.

While most of the current methods ignore the error sensitivity, one exception is that , which proposes to learn a text-image relation classifier to enhance multimodal BERT to reduce the interference from irrelevant images while requiring extensive annotation for the irrelevance of image-text pairs. Sun et al. (2020) introduce a visually grounded alignment objective for token-region interactions, Xu and Li (2021) study contrastive pretraining for cross-modal retrieval signals, Zhang et al. (2022) design prompt-based fusion over CLIP-like encoders, Wang et al. (2021) examine data augmentation with random image substitution, and Kim et al. (2020) report improvements on multimodal sarcasm detection. These advances motivate our focus on robust multimodal entity and relation extraction under noisy visual contexts.
spans: Sun et al. (2020) introduce a visually grounded alignment objective for token-region interactions, Xu and Li (2021) study contrastive pretraining for cross-modal retrieval signals, Zhang et al. (2022) design prompt-based fusion over CLIP-like encoders, Wang et al. (2021) examine data augmentation with random image substitution, and Kim et al. (2020) report improvements on multimodal sarcasm detection

Media Framing Bias Detection and Prediction Media bias has been studied extensively in various fields such as social science, economics, and political science, and various methods have been proposed to analyze the political preference and framing bias of news outlets (Groseclose and Milyo, 2005;Miller and Riechert, 2001;Park et al., 2011;Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010;Haselmayer and Jenny, 2017). Framing bias is selective reporting of an event to sway readers' opinions with different factors such as commission of extra information and word choices (Entman, 1993(Entman, , 2007Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006). In NLP, computational approaches for detecting media bias often consider linguistic cues that induce bias in political text (Recasens et al., 2013;Yano et al., 2010;Hamborg et al., 2019b). For instance, Gentzkow and Shapiro count the frequency of slanted words within articles. These methods mainly focus on the stylistic ("how to cover") aspect of framing bias. There is relatively less effort on the informational ("what to cover") aspect of framing bias (Park et al., 2011;Fan et al., 2019) and they are constrained to detection tasks. In this work, we attempt to tackle both by generating a bias-free summary out of biased headlines.

Media Bias Mitigation News aggregation by displaying articles from different news outlets on a particular topic (e.g., Google News 1 , Yahoo News 2 ), is the most common approach in NLP to mitigate media bias, but it still has limitations (Hamborg et al., 2019a). Other approaches have been proposed to provide additional information (Laban and Hearst, 2017), such as automatic classification of multiple view points (Park et al., 2009), multinational perspectives (Hamborg et al., 2017), and detailed media profiles (Zhang et al., 2019b). Google News 1 and Yahoo News 2 curate headlines algorithmically from many sources to increase coverage diversity. Prior work also examines agenda-setting differences across outlets and temporal dynamics of coverage (Boydstun et al., 2014; Roberts et al., 2020), and stance detection models that leverage cross-source signals (Küçük and Can, 2020). Metrics for exposure diversity and echo chambers are further discussed in the recommender systems literature (Nguyen et al., 2014; Chaney et al., 2018), with implications for personalization and filter bubbles. However, these methods focus on providing a broader perspective from an enlarged selection of articles to news readers, which still puts burden on the readers. We propose instead to automatically neutralize and summarize partisan headlines to produce a neutral headline summary.
spans: In NLP, computational approaches for detecting media bias often consider linguistic cues that induce bias in political text (Recasens et al., 2013;Yano et al., 2010;Hamborg et al., 2019b). For instance, Gentzkow and Shapiro count the frequency of slanted words within articles. These methods mainly focus on the stylistic ("how to cover") aspect of framing bias. There is relatively less effort on the informational ("what to cover") aspect of framing bias (Park et al., 2011;Fan et al., 2019) and they are constrained to detection tasks.

Other approaches have been proposed to provide additional information (Laban and Hearst, 2017), such as automatic classification of multiple view points (Park et al., 2009), multinational perspectives (Hamborg et al., 2017), and detailed media profiles (Zhang et al., 2019b). We also compare our method with previous SOTA work, where all referred systems exploit the pre-trained BERT model.
As shown, compared to Chen et al. (2020), our method performs better when using very limited training data.
Besides, our method can match their performance of semi-supervised setting that uses additional 10K unlabeled training data.
Moreover, our final model, without utilizing much external knowledge (e.g., document-level or manuallydesigned features), can achieve very competitive performance on the full training set in comparison to most of the previous systems.
Furthermore, it should be noted that achieving the absolute SOTA results of our model on the full training data is not the primary motivation of our work.
To contextualize these results, we review recent advances in data-efficient fine-tuning for BERT-based classifiers.
Chen et al. (2020) combine consistency regularization with pseudo-labeling to leverage limited supervision.
Subsequent studies refine these techniques with curriculum schedules and uncertainty-aware sampling to stabilize training under scarcity.
Our comparison further references Li and Wang (2019), Zhang et al. (2021), and Gupta et al. (2022) reporting improvements on related benchmarks, pretraining regimes with domain-adaptive objectives, and ablations on tokenization strategies across multilingual corpora.
In our setting, we isolate the impact of label efficiency by standardizing optimization hyperparameters and evaluation protocols.
We then analyze semi-supervised configurations that augment supervised examples with unlabeled pools drawn from the same domain.
The resulting gains align with prior observations that representation quality and calibration co-determine low-resource robustness.
To probe generalization, we evaluate cross-domain transfer from in-domain to near-domain distributions.
We observe that moderate domain shift primarily affects boundary cases with high lexical ambiguity.
Error analysis indicates that instance-level noise is amplified when pseudo-label confidence is not properly thresholded.
Ablation studies confirm that small architectural changes yield diminishing returns relative to improved sampling and regularization.
We also examine the role of feature sparsity and its interaction with class imbalance.
These results suggest that principled data selection is more valuable than extensive feature augmentation in our regime.
Taken together, the evidence supports our design choice to favor lean supervision over heavy external resources.
We conclude by outlining implications for scalable training under constrained annotation budgets.
spans: Our comparison further references Li and Wang (2019), Zhang et al. (2021), and Gupta et al. (2022) reporting improvements on related benchmarks, pretraining regimes with domain-adaptive objectives, and ablations on tokenization strategies across multilingual corpora.

document: Language model pre-training followed by task specific fine-tuning has proven to be a powerful tool for numerous NLP tasks Liu et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2019b;Radford et al., 2019;Raffel et al., 2019a;Lewis et al., 2020;Joshi et al., 2020). BERT introduced Mask Language Model (MLM), where a model predicts masked tokens given a sequence of text input. Fine-tuning a pre-trained BERT model has led to improved performance on various NLP tasks. However, MLM predictions are not made auto-regressively, which limits the capability of the BERT family for generation tasks. Raffel et al. (2019a) introduced the span corruption task in T5 as the pre-training objective, where a model predicts the masked token span using an autoregressive model. It can handle the generation tasks as the pretraining is done in a generative way. BART (Lewis et al., 2020) is similar to T5 but used a slightly different pre-training objective, in which spans are masked from the input but the complete output is predicted. However, none of these works tried to investigate pre-training for very long sequence inputs. They often use a transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture as backbone, the complexity of which is quadratic to the input length, making them impractical to model very long sequence input. More recent research has been focusing on improving the memory and computation efficiency of transformer models (Tay et al., 2020b(Tay et al., , 2021 for handling long input. One type of such approaches is using non-full attention patterns to restrict the attention field range, so that it reduces the attention complexity from O(n 2 ) to O(nlogn) or O(n), including Sinkhorn (Tay et al., 2020a), Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020), ETC (Ainslie et al., 2020), and BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020a). Another type of approaches is leveraging the low-rank approximation of the attention matrix, such as Linformer (Wang et al., 2020), Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021, Random Feature Attention (Peng et al., 2021), and LUNA (Ma et al., 2021). Recent work has also revisited retrieval-augmented generation for factuality (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Chen et al. (2017) proposed an open-domain QA pipeline that couples document retrieval with neural readers. Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced dense passage retrieval to improve evidence recall across large corpora. Izacard and Grave (2021) combined retrieval with sequence-to-sequence modeling to answer questions using multiple evidence passages. Despite these advances, long-input pre-training remains underexplored in terms of stability and scalability. Hierarchical transformers decompose long sequences into segments with local and global attention to improve tractability (Dai et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020). Memory-augmented architectures further extend context through explicit caches or recurrent states across segments (Grave et al., 2017; Rae et al., 2020). Position encoding innovations also mitigate extrapolation issues for long contexts by enabling length generalization (Press et al., 2021; Su et al., 2021). Concurrently, sparse mixture-of-experts increases capacity without quadratic cost growth and has been paired with long-context attention (Fedus et al., 2021; Lepikhin et al., 2021). Evaluation methodologies for long-context models emphasize streaming perplexity and retrieval-based probes that stress attention over thousands of tokens (Tay et al., 2022; Beltagy et al., 2020). Document-level summarization and multi-hop QA offer realistic stress tests of long-range dependency modeling (Fabbri et al., 2021; Khot et al., 2021). Our work contributes a pre-training scheme that aligns the objective with downstream span-sensitive generation while maintaining sub-quadratic complexity. We also provide an analysis of training dynamics under varying block lengths and segment overlap. Additionally, we compare attention pattern choices under identical compute budgets to quantify efficiency-quality trade-offs. Finally, we release reproducible setups and checkpoints to facilitate fair long-context pre-training comparisons.
spans: Recent work has also revisited retrieval-augmented generation for factuality (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Chen et al. (2017) proposed an open-domain QA pipeline that couples document retrieval with neural readers. Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced dense passage retrieval to improve evidence recall across large corpora. Izacard and Grave (2021) combined retrieval with sequence-to-sequence modeling to answer questions using multiple evidence passages.

document: Two major approaches are used for evaluating chatbots or dialogue systems. Some evaluate a single turn at a time by comparing the response with a ground truth utterance from a static script of real human dialogue or get a score by combining the response with the context. Others evaluate the interaction between human and bot or between bot and bot by some scoring metrics. Traditional metrics such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002b), ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) are widely used for evaluating text generation systems. More recently, automatic evaluation methods based on static scripts are gradually moving toward using pre-trained language models. Pang et al. (2020) uses GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) and Mehri and Eskenazi (2020) uses RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) to automatically evaluate the generation in an unsupervised way with a higher correlation to human evaluation than traditional ones.
spans: Traditional metrics such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002b), ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) are widely used for evaluating text generation systems. More recently, automatic evaluation methods based on static scripts are gradually moving toward using pre-trained language models. Pang et al. (2020) uses GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) and Mehri and Eskenazi (2020) uses RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) to automatically evaluate the generation in an unsupervised way with a higher correlation to human evaluation than traditional ones.
